title: Practice Exam #7 (Machine Learning Engineer)
description: Google Cloud Certified Professional Machine Learning Engineer - 274 questions

questions.text:
A logistics company is developing an internal assistant to summarize and annotate customer delivery feedback before support agents review it. The assistant needs to identify the type of item mentioned (such as perishable or fragile), estimate the likely time needed to resolve the issue, and determine the sentiment of the review. The final summary should be in plain language for internal teams.

The system uses the following approach:

First, customer feedback is published to a Pub/Sub topic. Then, Cloud Run service consumes the messages and calls three separate ML endpoints:

  • one to classify the item type
  • one to predict resolution time
  • one to analyze sentiment

The summarized output is displayed on an internal dashboard.

Which services should the Cloud Run service invoke for each endpoint?

options.text: Vertex AI for item classification, Vertex AI for time estimation, AutoML for sentiment analysis
options.explanation:
Vertex AI is the right choice for item classification and resolution time estimation because both tasks depend on the company’s specific data and categories. These are not general problems that can be handled well by pre-trained APIs. Vertex AI supports training and serving custom models that understand business-specific logic.

For sentiment analysis, the Cloud Natural Language API is a better option. It is a pre-trained service that works well for general text sentiment and does not require any custom training or infrastructure.

The options using AutoML for sentiment analysis involve more effort than necessary. AutoML can support text tasks, but you would need to train and host your own model. That adds complexity for a task that the Cloud Natural Language API already handles accurately out of the box.

The option that uses the Cloud Natural Language API for item classification is not appropriate because the API is not designed to detect custom item types like fragile or perishable. That kind of classification needs a model trained on specific data. The Cloud Vision API is also not useful here because it is for image content, not text.

Using the right combination of custom models for specific logic and pre-trained services for common tasks helps you build a simpler and more effective system.

questions.text:
A health and wellness app collects voice journals from users, which are stored as audio files in Cloud Storage. The company wants to transcribe these entries, remove any sensitive personal details, and analyze the emotional tone over time. The processed data must remain in the region where it was collected to comply with local data laws. Analysts use a third-party visualization tool that connects through a SQL ANSI-2011 compliant interface. You are asked to design a data pipeline that performs transcription, redacts personal information, and stores the output for analysis.

Which components should you choose for processing and analytics?

options.text: Use Dataflow for processing and Cloud SQL for analytics
options.explanation:
Dataflow is a serverless, scalable processing service that can handle large audio datasets, perform regional execution, and integrate with external APIs like Speech-to-Text and Data Loss Prevention. It is ideal for running a pipeline that transcribes audio and redacts personal information. BigQuery is the right choice for analytics because it supports large-scale querying, integrates well with external BI tools, and is ANSI SQL 2011 compliant.

Cloud SQL supports SQL but is not built for analytics at this scale and lacks the performance of BigQuery for large datasets. Firestore is a NoSQL document database, which does not provide the required SQL interface. Pub/Sub is a great ingestion tool but does not process or store data on its own. Cloud Run Functions is not ideal for large batch workflows.

options.text: Use Pub/Sub for ingestion and Firestore for analytics

questions.text:
An energy monitoring platform needs to track thousands of power grid sensors and detect unusual behavior as it happens. Sensor data is published continuously through Pub/Sub. You are building a system that must handle the stream, run an ML model to identify anomalies, and store the results so the operations team can explore trends and build dashboards. The solution must scale automatically and require minimal infrastructure management.

Which combination of services should you use?

options.text: BigQuery, Vertex AI, Cloud Storage
options.explanation:
This combination is the best fit for a system that processes streaming sensor data, performs machine learning inference, and supports exploration and visualization. Dataflow handles the continuous ingestion and transformation of data from Pub/Sub. Vertex AI can host a custom anomaly detection model and serve predictions in real time. BigQuery is well suited for storing the results because it allows easy aggregation, trend analysis, and integration with dashboarding tools.

Bigtable is often used with sensor data because it supports high-throughput writes and time series workloads. However, it is optimized for low-latency lookups and operational access patterns, not for analytical tasks. It does not support native querying for metrics like averages or counts across many devices and time windows. For this use case, where operations teams need to explore patterns and visualize insights, BigQuery is a better fit.

The other options fall short in different ways. Cloud Storage is not intended for structured querying. AutoML does not currently offer anomaly detection. Cloud Run Functions are not designed for sustained, high-volume stream processing. Firestore is a document database and does not scale well for analytical workloads. Dataproc adds unnecessary complexity for a streaming pipeline and is better suited to batch jobs, and does not integrate as natively with PubSub.

questions.text:
A university operates a campus shuttle system that transports students between dormitories and classroom buildings each morning. Students must reserve a seat by selecting their departure location and time slot using a mobile app built on Google Kubernetes Engine. Reservations close the night before. The operations team wants to minimize shuttle travel time and avoid unnecessary stops while ensuring all reserved passengers are picked up within vehicle capacity.

What should you do?

options.text: Train a classification model to predict which shuttle stops will have the highest student attendance, then send a shuttle along that route and skip unneeded stops
options.explanation:
Because students have already submitted confirmed reservations, you have exact information about who needs to be picked up and from where. There is no need to make predictions or simulate likely behavior. The goal is to create an efficient route that covers only the necessary stops while staying within shuttle capacity. This can be solved with standard routing logic, without introducing machine learning.

The other approaches assume that future behavior is uncertain and must be estimated from patterns. In this case, the data is complete and accurate ahead of time. Using classification, regression, or reinforcement learning would increase system complexity without improving outcomes. When confirmed inputs are available, the best use of resources is to optimize based directly on those inputs.

options.text: Develop a reinforcement learning policy that evaluates shuttle routes using historical student attendance, and use that policy to generate the day’s pickup sequence
options.explanation:
Because students have already submitted confirmed reservations, you have exact information about who needs to be picked up and from where. There is no need to make predictions or simulate likely behavior. The goal is to create an efficient route that covers only the necessary stops while staying within shuttle capacity. Using classification, regression, or reinforcement learning would increase system complexity without improving outcomes. When confirmed inputs are available, the best use of resources is to optimize based directly on those inputs.

options.text: Use a regression model trained on previous ridership data to estimate demand at each stop, then dispatch a shuttle based on expected load and predicted stops

The other approaches assume that future behavior is uncertain and must be estimated from patterns. In this case, the data is complete and accurate ahead of time. Using classification, regression, or reinforcement learning would increase system complexity without improving outcomes. When confirmed inputs are available, the best use of resources is to optimize based directly on those inputs.

options.text: Treat it as an optimization problem. Determine the most efficient route that includes only the confirmed stops while staying within capacity limits, and dispatch the appropriately sized shuttle accordingly
options.explanation:
Because students have already submitted confirmed reservations, you have exact information about who needs to be picked up and from where. There is no need to make predictions or simulate likely behavior. The goal is to create an efficient route that covers only the necessary stops while staying within shuttle capacity. This can be solved with standard routing logic, without introducing machine learning.

The other approaches assume that future behavior is uncertain and must be estimated from patterns. In this case, the data is complete and accurate ahead of time. Using classification, regression, or reinforcement learning would increase system complexity without improving outcomes. When confirmed inputs are available, the best use of resources is to optimize based directly on those inputs.

questions.text: A financial services company is training a model to detect suspicious account activity. The dataset contains millions of transactions, but only a small fraction are labeled as fraudulent. After multiple training runs, your classification model fails to converge or learn meaningful patterns. You need a strategy to address the imbalance in the dataset. What should you do?

options.text: Remove examples from the majority class until the classes are balanced
options.explanation:
When the minority class is too small, models often fail to learn from it. Downsampling the majority class helps by reducing the skew, and class weighting ensures the model still treats the minority class as important. This approach improves learning without discarding all useful data.

Equalizing the classes by removing too many negatives can hurt generalization.

Adding synthetic examples randomly may introduce noise. Synthetic data generation can be helpful when the minority class is underrepresented but still has enough structure to learn from.

A deeper model does not solve class imbalance and may make training harder with limited signal.

Downsampling with weighting is more reliable and efficient in this case.

options.text: Train a deep model with attention layers to better capture rare patterns
options.explanation:
When the minority class is too small, models often fail to learn from it. Downsampling the majority class helps by reducing the skew, and class weighting ensures the model still treats the minority class as important. This approach improves learning without discarding all useful data.

A deeper model does not solve class imbalance and may make training harder with limited signal.

Downsampling with weighting is more reliable and efficient in this case.

options.text: Downsample the majority class and apply class weighting during training
options.explanation:

Downsampling with weighting is more reliable and efficient in this case.

options.text: Generate synthetic fraud examples until both classes are equal

questions.text:
A media analytics company is running a daily batch pipeline to process structured log data stored in Cloud Storage. The current system uses PySpark on Dataproc to perform feature extraction and aggregations for machine learning, but the team is struggling with long pipeline run times and maintenance overhead. You want to rebuild the pipeline using a serverless service that supports SQL-based transformations and improves performance without requiring cluster management. Your team has already cleaned and staged the input files in Cloud Storage.

How should you design the new pipeline?

options.text: Load the data into Cloud SQL, convert the PySpark logic into SQL, and use federated queries in BigQuery for reporting and analysis
options.explanation:
BigQuery provides a serverless, high-performance analytics engine that supports SQL syntax and scales automatically. By loading the data into BigQuery and translating the transformation logic from PySpark into BigQuery SQL, the team can take advantage of a fully managed environment without needing to manage infrastructure. This approach improves development speed and significantly reduces pipeline execution time.

Cloud SQL is not designed for large-scale batch transformation workloads and would introduce unnecessary complexity when federated queries are used for compute-heavy tasks. Data Fusion is a viable ETL tool, but it adds orchestration overhead and is not as direct or performant for this use case. Continuing with Dataproc and SparkSQL keeps the same issues the team is trying to move away from, including cluster management and long runtimes. BigQuery is the only option here that meets all of the stated requirements.

options.text: Load the data into BigQuery, rewrite the PySpark logic using BigQuery SQL, and write the transformed data into a new BigQuery table
options.explanation:
BigQuery provides a serverless, high-performance analytics engine that supports SQL syntax and scales automatically. By loading the data into BigQuery and translating the transformation logic from PySpark into BigQuery SQL, the team can take advantage of a fully managed environment without needing to manage infrastructure. BigQuery is the only option here that meets all of the stated requirements.

options.text: Use Data Fusion pipelines to process the Cloud Storage files and output the results into a BigQuery dataset
options.explanation:

BigQuery is the only option here that meets all of the stated requirements.

options.text: Convert the PySpark code into SparkSQL and continue using Dataproc to run the batch job and store the output in BigQuery

questions.text:
A biotech startup has a team of researchers developing machine learning models using different tools, including TensorFlow, PyTorch, and specialized scientific packages. They currently use a manually configured cloud-based setup to submit training jobs, but managing dependencies and environments has become too time-consuming. The team needs a managed solution that allows each researcher to use their preferred framework and libraries while simplifying infrastructure operations.

What should you do?

options.text: Use Vertex AI custom containers to let researchers run training jobs with their preferred libraries on a fully managed platform
options.explanation:
Vertex AI custom containers allow teams to package any training code and dependencies into a container and run it on a fully managed infrastructure. This means researchers can continue using any combination of frameworks and tools without needing to rewrite their code. It also removes the burden of maintaining infrastructure or container orchestration systems.

Creating VM images for each configuration can work, but it leads to maintenance overhead and does not scale well. Kubeflow Pipelines requires more setup and often assumes TensorFlow-specific tooling unless extended. A Slurm-based job manager introduces additional infrastructure to manage and does not offer the same scalability or simplicity as a managed service. Vertex AI custom containers are the only option that combines flexibility with ease of use.

options.text: Create custom VM images for each environment on Compute Engine and allow researchers to launch training jobs using those VMs
options.explanation:
Vertex AI custom containers allow teams to package any training code and dependencies into a container and run it on a fully managed infrastructure. This means researchers can continue using any combination of frameworks and tools without needing to rewrite their code. It also removes the burden of maintaining infrastructure or container orchestration systems.

Creating VM images for each configuration can work, but it leads to maintenance overhead and does not scale well. Kubeflow Pipelines requires more setup and often assumes TensorFlow-specific tooling unless extended. A Slurm-based job manager introduces additional infrastructure to manage and does not offer the same scalability or simplicity as a managed service. Vertex AI custom containers are the only option that combines flexibility with ease of use.

options.text: Deploy Kubeflow Pipelines on Google Kubernetes Engine and require each team member to submit training jobs using KFP components

Your system is built on Google Cloud and uses Vertex AI pipelines for model training and deployment. The business regularly adds newly published books to its catalog. What should you do to maintain the relevance of your test dataset?

options.text: Keep the test dataset fixed so evaluation results remain consistent across versions
options.explanation:
To evaluate model performance effectively over time, your test dataset needs to reflect both the original content and the new data introduced during retraining. Adding new examples to the test set ensures that Vertex AI Model Monitoring can assess performance on both older and newer content. This helps track accuracy and detect potential degradation as your model adapts.

Keeping the test dataset fixed limits your ability to evaluate on new categories and may hide accuracy drops. Replacing the test dataset removes valuable historical examples and gives an incomplete view of model quality. Waiting until drift is detected delays updates that should be proactive. Expanding the test dataset is the best way to keep evaluation meaningful as your model evolves.

questions.text:
A retail analytics team is building demand forecasting models with Vertex AI. The team regularly updates the model logic in response to changing seasonal patterns and promotional campaigns. Their codebase is managed in GitLab, and they want every update to trigger retraining automatically so new models can be evaluated and deployed with minimal delay. They also want to avoid managing infrastructure and want to keep everything under version control.

What should you do?

options.text: Set up a Cloud Run Functions trigger that detects file changes in Cloud Storage and launches a training job on Vertex AI
options.explanation:
Cloud Build can be configured to watch a GitHub or GitLab repository and trigger a training workflow whenever code is updated. This approach is scalable, fully managed, and integrates naturally with version control systems. It ensures that your training jobs stay in sync with the latest code without needing manual steps or additional scheduling tools.

Using Cloud Run Functions or Cloud Composer for code monitoring introduces extra complexity and is less tightly integrated with Git. A local cron job running gcloud commands is fragile and difficult to scale across a team. Cloud Build offers a clean and automated solution with minimal overhead.

questions.text:
A financial services company is training a model on Vertex AI to classify scanned documents as tax forms, loan applications, or account statements. The label column contains integer values: 0 for tax forms, 1 for loan applications, and 2 for account statements. Each image belongs to exactly one of these categories. You want to use a loss function that works with integer-labeled data and avoids converting labels to one-hot vectors.

Which loss function should you use?

options.text: Binary cross-entropy
options.explanation:
Sparse categorical cross-entropy is the most suitable option for multi-class classification when your labels are integers rather than one-hot encoded vectors. It allows the model to directly interpret index-based class labels and simplifies preprocessing.

Hinge loss is used with support vector machines and is not common in deep learning classification tasks like this. Binary cross-entropy is for cases where each input may belong to multiple classes, which does not apply here. Categorical cross-entropy requires one-hot encoded labels, which makes it less efficient for this case. Sparse categorical cross-entropy fits the label format and classification goal exactly.

An online home goods store is using Recommendations AI to personalize product suggestions for shoppers. The business goal is to increase the total value of items purchased in a single transaction. You have already set up the data ingestion process and are ready to configure the recommendation type. The team asks for a solution that aligns with revenue growth rather than general user engagement.

Which recommendation type should you use?

options.text: Use the ‘Other Products You May Like’ recommendation type
options.explanation:
The ‘Frequently Bought Together’ recommendation type is designed to increase transaction size by suggesting items that are commonly purchased together. It directly supports a revenue objective by encouraging customers to add complementary products to their cart.

The ‘Other Products You May Like’ type is useful for increasing interaction, but it focuses on engagement and does not directly optimize for total purchase value. Importing user event data first may help model training but does not address the recommendation strategy. Using placeholder product data compromises the model’s reliability and violates best practices for data integrity. To increase revenue, the recommendation type must align with purchasing behavior rather than browsing behavior.

questions.text:
You are developing a machine learning model for a sports analytics platform to predict player injury risk using physiological and movement sensor data. Your model achieves excellent results on the training set but shows much higher error on the validation set. You want to improve generalization and reduce the chance of overfitting. You are using Vertex AI for training and want to follow best practices during retraining.

What should you do?

options.text: Decrease the learning rate and reduce the batch size to allow more detailed gradient updates
options.explanation:
When a model performs well on the training set but poorly on the validation set, it is likely overfitting. Dropout and L2 regularization are common and effective techniques to address this. Using Vertex AI to run hyperparameter tuning allows the system to find the best combination of these values without manual guesswork. This reduces overfitting in a scalable and repeatable way.

Manually setting values or increasing model complexity can make the problem worse. Lowering the learning rate and batch size may slow convergence but will not directly fix overfitting. Increasing training time without improving generalization will not help. The best strategy is to tune the regularization parameters systematically using a managed service like Vertex AI.

questions.text: You are managing a Tensorflow fraud detection model for a digital payments platform. After deployment, it performed well. Over time, false negatives have increased. The architecture hasn’t changed, and the data pipeline is functioning correctly. What most likely explains the drop in performance?

options.text: Outdated TensorFlow version
options.explanation: A model deployed in a dynamic environment like fraud detection is exposed to constantly shifting patterns in user behavior and fraud tactics. Even if the pipeline and model architecture are unchanged, these real-world changes lead to model drift. This means the data the model sees in production becomes less similar to the data it was trained on, resulting in a decline in predictive accuracy. Regular retraining with recent data helps the model adjust to new trends and maintain effectiveness. An outdated TensorFlow version may introduce compatibility or performance issues, but not gradual degradation in accuracy. Outliers in training data would typically cause immediate underperformance, not a delayed decline. A small test set affects evaluation reliability, but again, it wouldn't cause performance to worsen over time.

questions.text: You are training a TensorFlow model to classify satellite images stored in Cloud Storage. The dataset is too large to load into memory, and you need to build a scalable input pipeline that follows Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.text: Convert the images to TFRecord format, store them in Cloud Storage, and read them with the tf.data API
options.explanation: The recommended approach for large image datasets is to convert the data into TFRecords and use the tf.data API to read and preprocess the data. TFRecords provide an efficient binary format for large-scale datasets and allow data to be streamed from storage in batches, reducing memory pressure. The tf.data API supports operations like parallel reading and prefetching, but prefetch is only useful after the data is already in the pipeline. It cannot address the challenge of data that doesn’t fit in memory. Generators and raw reads lack the efficiency and scalability of tf.data pipelines. Importing the data directly without an input pipeline would not solve the problem because that's what's creating the problem in the first place.

options.text: Write a generator function that loads images on the fly using tf.io.read_file()
options.explanation: The recommended approach for large image datasets is to convert the data into TFRecords and use the tf.data API to read and preprocess the data. TFRecords provide an efficient binary format for large-scale datasets and allow data to be streamed from storage in batches, reducing memory pressure. The tf.data API supports operations like parallel reading and prefetching, but prefetch is only useful after the data is already in the pipeline. It cannot address the challenge of data that doesn’t fit in memory. Generators and raw reads lack the efficiency and scalability of tf.data pipelines. Importing the data directly without an input pipeline would not solve the problem because that's what's creating the problem in the first place.

options.text: Use tf.data.Dataset.prefetch() to load the dataset into memory before training
options.explanation: The recommended approach for large image datasets is to convert the data into TFRecords and use the tf.data API to read and preprocess the data. TFRecords provide an efficient binary format for large-scale datasets and allow data to be streamed from storage in batches, reducing memory pressure. The tf.data API supports operations like parallel reading and prefetching, but prefetch is only useful after the data is already in the pipeline. It cannot address the challenge of data that doesn’t fit in memory. Generators and raw reads lack the efficiency and scalability of tf.data pipelines. Importing the data directly without an input pipeline would not solve the problem because that's what's creating the problem in the first place.

questions.text:
You are building a machine learning model for a large e-commerce company that wants to forecast daily sales for thousands of products across different categories. The model needs to take into account regional sales history, promotional campaigns, seasonal trends, and product popularity over time. The company updates its data every night and expects the model to adjust its predictions based on new incoming data.

Which type of model is most appropriate for this use case?

options.text: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)
options.explanation:
Recurrent Neural Networks are designed to handle data where the order of inputs matters, such as time series. In this case, the model needs to process sales data over time and adapt to changes in daily inputs. RNNs are suited for identifying temporal patterns like seasonality or trends, which are critical in forecasting applications.

CNNs are powerful for analyzing spatial patterns in image data, but they are not built to capture dependencies across time steps.

AutoML for image classification focuses on classifying visual inputs and does not address time series predictions.

K-Means is used for grouping similar data points without any notion of time or prediction, so it does not help with forecasting.

questions.text: A health analytics startup is developing a real-time pipeline to ingest clinical lab reports from multiple providers into Google Cloud. These reports may contain sensitive health information and personal identifiers. The team wants to inspect the reports using the Cloud Data Loss Prevention (DLP) API before any data is accessed by analytics teams or stored in BigQuery. You have to determine whether the data is sensitive or is approved for use. What is the best approach to protect sensitive data from being viewed by unauthorized individuals?

options.text: Store all incoming reports in a Quarantine bucket. Use the DLP API to scan and classify each file, then move it to either a Sensitive or Approved bucket based on the result.
options.explanation:
Storing incoming data in a Quarantine bucket allows for complete control over data access before any processing or analytics occur. This ensures that sensitive or regulated content is never exposed to users or services before inspection. The DLP API can classify the data safely, and files can be routed to appropriate destinations only after review.

Loading data into BigQuery first or streaming through Pub/Sub before classification creates a risk of premature access. Even if scanning happens quickly afterward, there is still a window where unreviewed data could be exposed. Labeling a bucket as Approved without prior inspection goes against standard controls, since sensitive content may already be in that location. Scanning after the fact is too late to guarantee protection.

options.text: Ingest the reports into a Cloud Storage bucket, then immediately trigger a Cloud Run Function to load the data into BigQuery. Scan the BigQuery tables later with the DLP API.
options.explanation:

Scanning after the fact is too late to guarantee protection.

questions.text:
You work for a financial technology startup that offers credit risk scoring services. The product team wants to use machine learning to predict a customer’s default risk over the next 60 days based on their transaction patterns. The data is stored in BigQuery and includes timestamps for purchases, missed payments, and account updates. You are preparing the dataset to train a model using AutoML.

How should you structure the data to help AutoML generate the most reliable predictions?

options.text: Upload the dataset without any changes and allow AutoML to perform a random train-test split. Let it handle all transformations automatically.
options.explanation:
When a time signal exists, you should indicate a time column during AutoML setup. This helps AutoML avoid random splits that might mix past and future data, which can cause data leakage. A proper time-aware split ensures the model is tested on data that comes after the training window, which better reflects how it will perform in production.

Letting AutoML do a random split without a time column ignores the sequence of events and can lead to unrealistic results. Combining timestamp fields into a string prevents AutoML from understanding temporal patterns. Manually tagging data outside the platform is risky and bypasses built-in time-based splitting, which can lead to inconsistent outcomes or setup errors.

options.text: Combine all timestamp features into a single formatted string before upload, and rely on AutoML to infer the correct order and relationships.

questions.text:
You are part of a data science team at a transportation analytics company. Your team maintains a repository of code for training and deploying custom Vertex AI models that predict traffic patterns. These models use a set of custom preprocessing libraries. You want to ensure that every time someone pushes code to the “dev” branch in Cloud Source Repositories, all unit tests run automatically to validate that the pipeline and dependencies still function correctly.

What should you do?

options.text: Use Cloud Scheduler to periodically run a test job on Cloud Run that pulls the latest code from the dev branch and runs unit tests.
options.explanation:
Cloud Build is designed for CI/CD tasks such as testing and building ML pipelines. It allows you to define triggers that respond to pushes to specific branches. In this case, a Cloud Build trigger will monitor the dev branch and automatically run your test suite each time changes are made. This ensures that new commits are validated right away and helps catch breakages early.

Using Cloud Scheduler introduces delay and cannot guarantee that tests run with each push. Pub/Sub with Cloud Run Functions adds more complexity than needed, and Cloud Logging sinks are not the most direct way to monitor code updates. Running tests manually in Cloud Shell does not provide automation or consistency and is prone to human error.

options.text: Use Cloud Build to set up a trigger that automatically runs the unit tests each time changes are pushed to the dev branch.

questions.text:
You are training a Transformer-based model on Vertex AI to classify customer support tickets into priority tiers. You are using the following job submission script:

gcloud ai custom-jobs create \

  --region=us-central1 \

  --display-name=priority-model-training \

  --config=model_train.yaml

The model_train.yaml file includes:

workerPoolSpecs:

  - machineSpec:

      machineType: n1-standard-8

    replicaCount: 1

    containerSpec:

      imageUri: gcr.io/my-project/support-ticket-trainer

      args:

        - --epochs=25

        - --batch_size=64

        - --learning_rate=0.0005

You want to shorten training time while maintaining model performance. What should you do?

options.text: Change the learning rate to a higher value
options.explanation:
Upgrading the compute resources, such as using more powerful machines or adding GPUs, speeds up the training process without altering the model’s configuration or behavior. This approach allows the model to complete the same number of training steps in less time by leveraging more hardware processing power.

Increasing the learning rate may reduce training time, but it can make the model unstable or less accurate. Reducing epochs can prevent the model from fully learning the patterns in the data. Increasing batch size affects memory usage and may impact convergence behavior. These changes can shorten training, but they introduce tradeoffs that can hurt final performance.

questions.text:
Your company runs an online retail platform that uses a product recommendation model deployed on Vertex AI. You have released several updated versions of the model over the last three months, and you want to track which version performs best over time based on user engagement. You want to measure prediction quality on real traffic and ensure you detect any degradation in performance.

How should you compare the performance of the model versions?

options.text: Use BigQuery to compare training loss across model versions and identify which had the best fit during development.
options.explanation:
Mean average precision is a strong metric for evaluating performance in recommendation or ranking tasks, especially when using real-time prediction results. Vertex AI Model Monitoring supports monitoring this metric and allows you to compare different model versions on live data.

Training loss provides no insight into real-world performance and is better suited for development-stage evaluation. Mean squared error is typically used for regression tasks, not for recommendation models that rely on ranking relevance. While using Model Monitoring is a good idea, tracking the wrong metric will give misleading results. The What-If Tool is interactive and helpful for exploration, but not suitable for long-term performance tracking in production.

options.text: Use Vertex AI Model Monitoring to track and compare mean squared error (MSE) across model versions.

questions.text:
You trained a question-answer matching model that takes in pairs of text inputs: a customer query and a potential FAQ answer. You deployed the model using TensorFlow Serving and exposed the prediction endpoint. The model expects input as a batch of string pairs, and the SignatureDef indicates the input shape is (-1, 2) with DT_STRING and the output shape is (-1, 1) with DT_FLOAT.

You want to send a request to the model’s REST endpoint to check predictions for several query-answer pairs. You use the following setup:

headers = {"content-type": "application/json"}
response = requests.post(
    "http://localhost:8501/v1/models/qa_match_model:predict",
    data=data,
    headers=headers
)

What is the correct way to construct the data payload?

options.text: data = json.dumps({ "signature_name": "serving_default", "instances": [["query1"], ["answer1"], ["query2"], ["answer2"]] })
options.explanation:
The model expects input in batches where each instance contains two string values. This is specified by the shape [-1, 2]. Each sublist inside the instances array must include exactly two elements: the query and the corresponding answer. The correct format matches this expectation by submitting a list of two-element lists.

Providing inputs as a flat list of strings or as nested lists with the wrong number of elements will not match the model's input signature and will cause a shape mismatch error. Including more than two strings per instance also violates the required input structure and will result in a failed prediction request.

questions.text:
You are working as a machine learning engineer for a streaming service. The product team wants to improve the recommendation engine so that users are shown new shows or movies based on their watch history and preferences of users with similar behavior. You are asked to design a model to support this initiative.

What type of model should you build?

options.text: Collaborative filtering model
options.explanation:
Collaborative filtering is well suited for recommending items based on past user behavior and similarities across users. It does not require detailed metadata about the content itself. Instead, it focuses on patterns in what users have interacted with and what similar users have liked or watched.

A regression model could predict ratings, but it would rely heavily on labeled data and content features, and it would not fully capture user-to-user similarity. A knowledge-based filtering model helps when recommendations are based on explicit content relationships, not patterns in user behavior. Rule-based systems rely on heuristics or popularity but do not personalize results based on individual watch history.

options.text: Regression model
options.explanation:
Collaborative filtering is well suited for recommending items based on past user behavior and similarities across users. It does not require detailed metadata about the content itself. Instead, it focuses on patterns in what users have interacted with and what similar users have liked or watched.

A knowledge-based filtering model helps when recommendations are based on explicit content relationships, not patterns in user behavior. Rule-based systems rely on heuristics or popularity but do not personalize results based on individual watch history.

questions.text:
You work for an e-commerce company that uses a machine learning model to flag potentially fake product reviews. The model has been deployed to Vertex AI Prediction and is evaluated with a live feedback loop. The trust and safety team reports that too many legitimate reviews are being flagged incorrectly. When you check the evaluation results, you confirm that the model’s precision is too low.

To improve precision, how should you adjust the model’s softmax threshold?

options.text: Increase the softmax threshold to reduce recall
options.explanation:
When you raise the softmax threshold, the model becomes more conservative in labeling something as fake. It only flags reviews when it is highly confident. This reduces false positives, which increases precision. The tradeoff is that you’ll miss more actual fake reviews, which lowers recall.

Lowering the threshold or increasing the number of flagged reviews would increase recall but at the cost of precision. Optimizing for recall without regard to the threshold moves in the wrong direction when the concern is about flagging too many legitimate reviews.

options.text: Decrease the softmax threshold to classify more reviews as fake

You are leading a data modernization project for a retail company that wants to integrate data from legacy warehouses, cloud storage, and real-time transaction logs. The current setup involves manual scripts and custom connectors, which have led to inconsistent transformations, high maintenance costs, and poor visibility into data flows. Your team is looking for a solution that is fully managed, works well with hybrid sources, and provides a codeless way to design and manage ETL pipelines.

Which Google Cloud service should you use?

options.text: Cloud Composer
options.explanation:
Data Fusion is built for managing ETL and ELT workflows across diverse data environments. It supports integration with on-premises systems, offers a codeless interface, and simplifies pipeline development and monitoring. This makes it well-suited for unifying fragmented systems in modernization efforts.

Cloud Composer is primarily an orchestration tool and does not provide ETL logic out of the box. Dataproc is good for custom processing using Hadoop or Spark, but it requires more manual work. Storage Transfer Service is useful for transferring data, not for transforming or integrating it in pipeline workflows.

questions.text: You are an ML engineer at a financial institution that issues consumer loans. You are tasked with building a machine learning model that predicts whether to approve or deny loan applications. Because the model will directly impact customers and is subject to regulatory oversight, you must consider compliance requirements before development. Which factors should you think about first?

options.text: Traceability, reproducibility, and explainability
options.explanation:
In regulated sectors like lending, traceability, reproducibility, and explainability are essential. Traceability ensures that model outputs can be linked to input features and logic used. Reproducibility supports consistent outcomes under the same conditions. Explainability helps both regulators and applicants understand why a decision was made.

The other answer choices mention important concepts, but they do not meet the core compliance needs. Data encryption and privacy protect information but do not support auditability. Latency and model size affect performance, not transparency. Federated learning is useful for distributed systems but is not central to regulated decision-making. Fairness is important but not sufficient on its own without traceability and reproducibility.

questions.text:
You are training an image classification model on Vertex AI using TPUs to identify manufacturing defects in high-resolution camera footage. After reviewing the Cloud TPU profiler output, you determine that the training process is heavily input-bound and the TPU is underutilized. You want to optimize the data input pipeline using the tf.data API to reduce idle time and improve throughput.

Which changes should you apply to your tf.data pipeline?

(Choose two.)

options.text: Reduce the number of dataset repetitions per epoch
options.explanation:
To reduce input bottlenecks on TPUs, you should improve how data is read and prepared for training. Interleaving enables parallel reads from multiple sources, helping prevent delays caused by sequential access. Prefetching allows the pipeline to prepare the next batch while the current one is being processed. Together, these changes ensure that data is available quickly enough to keep the TPU busy.

Reducing repetitions has no direct impact on input speed. Increasing the shuffle buffer size might improve randomness, but does not address throughput. Reducing image resolution changes the input structure and may affect model performance rather than improving input pipeline efficiency.

questions.text:
You are building an ML pipeline for a retail company that uses real-time customer behavior data to generate personalized product recommendations. Your model was trained on extensively preprocessed features, and that same preprocessing must be performed consistently at prediction time. You have deployed the model to Vertex AI and need to support high-throughput online inference with minimal latency.

Which architecture should you use?

options.text: Retrain the model on raw data and deploy it directly to Vertex AI for prediction
options.explanation:
Using Pub/Sub and Dataflow is the most scalable and efficient way to preprocess data before calling a Vertex AI endpoint. Dataflow handles computationally intensive transformations well and integrates seamlessly with Pub/Sub for asynchronous high-throughput systems.

Cloud SQL with scheduled queries introduces latency and does not support real-time streaming. Cloud Run is not optimal for heavy or complex preprocessing workloads at scale. Retraining the model to work on raw data may reduce accuracy or consistency, and does not address the original architecture requirement.

questions.text:
You work for a telecom provider that uses a regression model to forecast customer call volume at each service center. The model was trained and validated with accurate historical data, but several months after deployment, it begins to produce inaccurate predictions. You discover that the patterns in the incoming data no longer match the patterns in the training set.

What should you do to address this issue?

options.text: Select new hyperparameters using a tuning service and retrain the model
options.explanation:
Changes in input patterns over time can lead to data skew, which causes a model trained on past data to perform poorly in production. The most effective approach is to monitor for skew between the training and serving data. Once skew is detected, retraining the model on updated data ensures it stays aligned with current trends.

Hyperparameter tuning and feature pruning are useful in some cases, but they do not address the root issue when the data distribution has shifted. Batch normalization affects training dynamics but does not solve problems caused by distribution drift between training and prediction.

options.text: Set up skew monitoring and retrain the model using recent data

questions.text:
Your company is building a mobile platform for a large gym chain that supports millions of users. You’ve developed a model that predicts whether users will miss their next scheduled workout based on patterns in app activity and location data. You want to send users a push notification if the model predicts that they are likely to skip.

How should you serve your predictions and send notifications to users?

options.text: Create an individual Pub/Sub topic for every user. Deploy a Cloud Run service that sends a notification when the model forecasts the user will likely skip a workout that day
options.explanation:
The best solution is to use Firebase Cloud Messaging with individual user IDs in a Firebase instance. This setup lets you send direct, real-time alerts to each user when the model predicts they are likely to miss their workout. FCM is built for scalable, targeted messaging to both mobile and web apps.

Creating a Pub/Sub topic per user is not scalable and adds operational overhead. Using App Engine increases complexity without adding targeting advantages. Sending alerts based on system-wide averages doesn't meet the requirement of user-level prediction and notification.

questions.text:
You are part of a data science team at a healthcare analytics company. Your team recently ingested 800 MB of hospital readmission data into BigQuery. You want to explore the dataset and conduct exploratory analysis using pandas in a Vertex AI Workbench notebook. 

What should you do?

options.text: Export the table from BigQuery to Google Sheets, and then use the Google Sheets API to read the data into the notebook as a dataframe
options.explanation:
The best option is to use BigQuery cell magic in Vertex AI Workbench Notebooks. This method allows you to run SQL queries inline with notebook cells using %%bigquery and returns the results directly as a pandas dataframe, with no additional exports or file handling.

Exporting to Google Sheets or a local CSV file adds unnecessary steps and complexity. The gsutil and bash route also works but is more manual and error-prone. For interactive data exploration in notebooks, the BigQuery cell magic is designed to streamline the workflow.

questions.text: You are building an ML model for a global restaurant review platform. Your goal is to predict review volume based on restaurant type in different urban areas. The training dataset includes binned latitude and longitude to represent location, and one-hot encoded restaurant types. How should you construct your features to best model the relationship between restaurant type and review volume in each area?

options.text: Use three separate features: binned latitude, binned longitude, and one-hot encoded restaurant type
options.explanation:
The best choice is to create one combined feature that crosses binned latitude, binned longitude, and restaurant type. This produces a single identifier for each city-zone and restaurant type pairing, helping the model distinguish spatially specific patterns.

Using individual features does not encode the interaction between location and type. Multiplying raw coordinates with restaurant types produces values with no clear meaning. Making two partial crosses splits the spatial context and does not allow the model to see full geographic combinations. A single cross over all three features better captures localized variation in review volume.

options.text: Use the product of raw latitude, raw longitude, and restaurant type
options.explanation:

questions.text: You are working with a legal firm that wants to build a system to automatically route incoming client emails to the right legal department based on the content of each message. The emails have already been ingested into Cloud Storage and converted to plain text. You want to develop a classification model that can identify the appropriate department (e.g., family law, corporate, immigration) for each email. You need to minimize manual preprocessing and custom code. What should you do?

options.text: Use the Cloud Natural Language API to extract entities and classify based on predefined types
options.explanation:
AutoML is the most efficient approach here. It supports custom text classification using labeled examples and does not require writing model code or building preprocessing pipelines. This aligns with the goal of minimizing setup and engineering work.

The Cloud Natural Language API cannot support custom categories because it only detects predefined entities. Vertex AI built-in containers allow custom modeling but involve more setup and configuration. Keyword matching would be brittle and lacks the generalization and accuracy of a trained model. AutoML allows training directly on labeled text examples and is suited for this use case.

options.text: Build a model using Vertex AI built-in training containers for text classification
options.explanation:
AutoML is the most efficient approach here. It supports custom text classification using labeled examples and does not require writing model code or building preprocessing pipelines. This aligns with the goal of minimizing setup and engineering work.

The Cloud Natural Language API cannot support custom categories because it only detects predefined entities. Vertex AI built-in containers allow custom modeling but involve more setup and configuration. Keyword matching would be brittle and lacks the generalization and accuracy of a trained model. questions.text: Your team is developing a fraud detection system for a global payment processing company. You are using TensorFlow to train a binary classifier on 80 billion transaction records stored in multiple CSV files. During training, your data pipeline is becoming a bottleneck and slowing down your experiments. You need to increase data throughput during training. What should you do?

options.text: Load the CSV files into BigQuery, then access them from your training job using SQL queries
options.explanation:
TFRecord format is designed to support efficient binary encoding and fast sequential reading, which makes it suitable for large-scale TensorFlow training jobs. Using TFRecords avoids the overhead of CSV parsing and integrates smoothly with tf.data pipelines. Storing the shards in Cloud Storage provides a scalable and highly available backend that supports parallel access across distributed training jobs.

BigQuery is built for analytics and SQL-style querying rather than high-performance training input. Cloud Bigtable is optimized for low-latency point reads and does not offer the throughput or compatibility needed for model training pipelines. HDFS is not native to Google Cloud and adds unnecessary infrastructure and maintenance overhead.

questions.text: You are an ML engineer working for a logistics company that receives thousands of shipment status updates throughout the day. At the end of each day, your team generates a summary file that includes all delivery event data for that day. You have trained a TensorFlow model on Vertex AI to detect patterns that may indicate delivery issues. You want to use this model every evening to analyze the new data without requiring manual input. What should you do?

options.text: Run the model on-demand using Vertex AI online prediction every time the file is generated
options.explanation:
Batch prediction is the right choice when you want to run inference on a large dataset at scheduled times without needing real-time or event-driven processing. In this scenario, the data is collected and processed in a single file at the end of each day, making batch inference efficient and easy to automate. Vertex AI's batch prediction feature lets you submit the file and process results in one step without maintaining infrastructure.

Online prediction and Cloud Run Functions are designed for real-time, low-latency scenarios where each prediction must happen immediately after data arrives. Deploying your own pipeline on Compute Engine works but requires more effort to manage infrastructure and scheduling. Batch prediction avoids those concerns and is purpose-built for high-throughput inference on static datasets.

questions.text: You are working on a new ML initiative at a healthcare research institute that manages tens of thousands of clinical datasets in BigQuery. The datasets are well-documented, and each table has a detailed description. You need to locate specific tables that mention drug resistance to feed into a Vertex AI pipeline. What is the most efficient way to find the tables you need?

options.text: Keep a spreadsheet of dataset descriptions and manually search for the correct table names
options.explanation:
When working with a large number of well-documented BigQuery datasets, the most effective way to search by content or metadata is to use Data Catalog. It lets you search across datasets using keywords, tags, and descriptions without needing to manually scan or curate mappings. In this case, searching for “drug resistance” will return tables with that term in the description.

Using a spreadsheet or a custom lookup table adds unnecessary manual effort and doesn’t scale well. INFORMATION_SCHEMA views provide metadata like table names and schemas but not the full descriptive context, so they are not suitable for keyword-based discovery. Data Catalog is purpose-built for this kind of metadata search and is integrated with BigQuery, which makes it the right choice for this situation.

questions.text: You are helping a logistics company build a model to forecast delivery delays using historical package data. You achieve a training AUC of 98% within your first few experiments, despite using a simple logistic regression model and minimal feature engineering. You haven't yet tuned the model or introduced any regularization. What should you do next to validate your results?

options.text: Replace the current model with a more constrained one like Naive Bayes and compare results
options.explanation:
When a simple model reaches near-perfect performance right away, it's often a sign that the model has access to leaked information. Data leakage can occur when a feature contains data that wouldn’t realistically be available at prediction time or is too closely tied to the label. In this case, some feature might be capturing downstream or proxy information, such as a late scan timestamp or customer complaint that correlates directly with delay outcomes.

Rather than jumping into tuning or model simplification, your first step should be to review the data pipeline and feature set. Removing features that are highly correlated with the target reduces leakage risk and gives you a better sense of the model’s true generalization performance. Techniques like cross-validation can help assess stability but won't fix the root issue if leakage exists. Likewise, hyperparameter tuning won't solve the fundamental problem of invalid input data.

options.text: Investigate the features and remove any that are strongly correlated with whether a delivery was late

questions.text:
You are supporting an e-commerce company that wants to personalize product recommendations based on a user’s real-time browsing behavior. The recommendations are generated by a trained model hosted on Vertex AI. The company requires that predictions meet a latency target of 250ms at the 99th percentile and that all requests go through an authenticated gateway. The current site handles millions of requests daily, and the engineering team prefers a setup that avoids unnecessary operational complexity.

How should you architect the system?

options.text: Route client requests through Cloud Load Balancing to a Compute Engine backend that forwards them to Vertex AI Prediction
options.explanation:
The best solution is to embed the client on the site, forward the requests through an App Engine backend, and call the model on Vertex AI Prediction. This approach ensures authenticated communication while keeping the architecture lightweight. Vertex AI Prediction can serve low-latency predictions at scale without the need for container management, and App Engine provides an easy way to host a secure gateway layer.

Using Compute Engine or GKE introduces additional infrastructure and management effort, which isn’t needed when App Engine and Vertex AI meet the latency and scalability needs. Firestore and Cloud Run add extra components and indirection, increasing the chance of latency spikes. Pub/Sub is not designed for real-time inference pipelines. Memorystore may improve access to session data, but it adds complexity and GKE introduces unnecessary overhead for a use case that doesn’t require custom containers or orchestration.

options.text: Embed the client in the browser, forward requests through a backend on App Engine, and call the model hosted on Vertex AI Prediction
options.explanation:

questions.text:
Your team is developing a ResNet-based image classification model using PyTorch. You've been running training jobs on local CPUs, but the experiments are taking too long to complete. You decide to move to Google Cloud to improve iteration speed. Your code does not include any custom device placement logic and does not use TPUStrategy. You want a setup that will accelerate training without requiring rework or complex setup.

Which training environment should you choose?

options.text: Set up a Compute Engine VM with a TPU and install all dependencies manually
options.explanation:
A Deep Learning VM with a GPU and pre-installed PyTorch libraries gives you hardware acceleration with minimal setup. This option supports standard training code out of the box, without needing device placement or compilation strategies for TPUs. It also avoids the overhead of managing containers or GKE clusters.

The TPU option is not a fit because TPUs require code that is TPU-compatible, such as TPUStrategy. A GKE cluster introduces complexity in managing resources and building container images, which is unnecessary for standalone training. Additionally, having multiple GPUs is not what you should start with - you should start small and make your way up.

A high-CPU VM without a GPU won't solve the performance bottleneck since PyTorch CNNs benefit heavily from GPU acceleration.

options.text: Create a custom GKE cluster with multiple GPU nodes and build your own container image
options.explanation:
A Deep Learning VM with a GPU and pre-installed PyTorch libraries gives you hardware acceleration with minimal setup. This option supports standard training code out of the box, without needing device placement or compilation strategies for TPUs. It also avoids the overhead of managing containers or GKE clusters.

questions.text:
You’ve recently joined a machine learning operations team at a large company that has multiple business units building and deploying models on Vertex AI. Each unit owns dozens of jobs, models, endpoints, and experiments. Some resources are shared across teams, and others are owned by a single group. You’ve been asked to help set up a consistent way to organize these resources for easier filtering and tracking across the organization.

What should you recommend?

options.text: Use resource labels to group resources based on team ownership, lifecycle stage, and project name
options.explanation:
Using labels is the most effective way to organize Vertex AI resources across teams without splitting projects or duplicating infrastructure. Labels can capture relevant attributes like ownership, stage, or use case. This allows teams to filter by label in the console or via APIs, monitor usage, and generate reports.

Relying on BigQuery logs requires post-processing and doesn't make filtering or organizing easier during day-to-day operations. Managing everything through service accounts focuses on access control, not resource visibility. Creating separate notebook instances tied to IAM groups is focused on restricting use rather than organizing the broader Vertex AI landscape. Labels are the cleanest and most scalable way to categorize resources.

questions.text:
Your team is preparing to launch a deep learning training job using a Deep Learning VM with a specific GPU type. When configuring the VM, the deployment fails with an error indicating that the accelerator type is not found.

What should you do to resolve the issue?

options.text: Increase the GPU memory limit in your training script to prevent allocation errors
options.explanation: The error message that the accelerator type was not found usually means that the selected GPU is not available in that particular region or zone. This is not a quota issue, nor is it caused by insufficient memory or CPU resources. Google Cloud provides different types of GPUs in different zones, and availability can vary. The correct action is to verify which GPU types are supported in the desired location and either adjust the region or select a different supported GPU type. Memory limits and machine type are not the root cause in this situation.

questions.text:
Your analytics team has completed development on an ML-based recommendation engine that will be integrated into your company’s customer-facing app. As the release date approaches, you’re asked to conduct a final checklist to verify that the ML components are ready for production. Data validation, model training, and deployment infrastructure have already passed testing.

What additional step should you prioritize to ensure ongoing reliability after deployment?

options.text: Ensure that model monitoring is in place
options.explanation:
The correct step is to implement model monitoring before you deploy. This is critical in production environments because changes in user behavior, data pipelines, or feature distributions can cause model performance to degrade even if everything worked well in testing. Monitoring enables early detection of such problems so they can be corrected before impacting users.

Scheduling regular training runs helps with retraining, but without monitoring, you may not know when retraining is needed. Ensuring normalization and input prep are valid concerns, but they belong earlier in the pipeline and are usually validated before training. Monitoring is the step that bridges testing and long-term reliability in production.

questions.text:
Your team is building a machine learning system to categorize product images for a retail application. You are using TensorFlow with a GPU-backed Deep Learning VM on Compute Engine. The model input size is 256×256 pixels, and you have set the batch size to 128. The training process uses the Adam optimizer and runs for 20 epochs.

When you start the training job, it fails with a ResourceExhaustedError due to an out-of-memory (OOM) issue on the GPU. What change should you make first to resolve the issue?

options.text: Change the learning rate
options.explanation: A ResourceExhaustedError typically happens when the model tries to load more data into GPU memory than it can handle during a single training step. The batch size determines how much data is processed at once, so lowering the batch size directly reduces memory usage and is usually the most effective first step. Adjusting the learning rate or switching optimizers will not address memory allocation. Reducing image size could help, but it may unnecessarily impact model performance and should be a secondary consideration.

questions.text: You are deploying a trained image classification model on GKE using TensorFlow Serving. The model is running in a production environment on CPU-only nodes and is receiving thousands of prediction requests every second. The current response times are too slow, but you cannot change the infrastructure configuration, add GPUs, or redesign the model architecture. What should you do to improve latency?

options.text: Switch to a version of TensorFlow Serving that includes universal binary support
options.explanation: To improve latency without modifying infrastructure or switching to GPU-based nodes, your best option is to recompile TensorFlow Serving with CPU-specific optimizations, such as AVX2 or AVX-512, and pair that with an appropriate CPU platform selection in GKE. This lets the model inference code take full advantage of the available hardware. Simply changing batch or timeout parameters does not address the fundamental processing inefficiencies on CPU. Using a universal binary version of TensorFlow Serving avoids compatibility issues but does not optimize for performance on a specific CPU type.

questions.text: Your analytics team processes weekly reports that rely on historical e-commerce data stored in BigQuery. Previously, you used Dataflow to normalize price and quantity values before sending them to a dashboarding tool. The team has grown and wants to streamline operations by reducing pipeline steps and avoiding unnecessary data movement. What should you do to simplify the pipeline while maintaining accuracy?

options.text: Convert the normalization logic into SQL statements executed directly within BigQuery
options.explanation: Since your data is already stored in BigQuery, the most efficient approach is to handle preprocessing inside BigQuery using SQL. Writing the normalization logic as SQL expressions avoids the overhead of external tools and keeps the process simple and repeatable. This also helps avoid latency introduced by moving data between services. Using TensorFlow Transform or Cloud Run may be helpful for model input pipelines or inference scenarios, but they are not the most efficient way to preprocess bulk data stored in BigQuery. Similarly, Dataproc adds unnecessary infrastructure overhead when SQL can handle the same computation in place.

questions.text: You are training a regression model to forecast energy demand in residential buildings. The dataset includes hourly meter readings from the past year. You randomly split the data into training and test sets and apply normalization before training. During evaluation, the model shows strong performance, but once deployed in production, it often gives inaccurate results on live data. You want to improve how well the test performance reflects real-world behavior. What should you do?

options.text: Increase the size of the test set and sample from a broader range of meter values across the dataset
options.explanation: For time series data, random splits can lead to leakage from future data into the training set, which results in misleading evaluation metrics. A chronological split avoids this by simulating the conditions the model will face in production. It ensures that the model is trained only on past data and tested on future data, which better matches the way it will be used in practice. Enlarging the test set or adjusting normalization helps with other concerns but does not address this type of leakage. Monitoring production performance is useful, but it doesn’t solve the mismatch between training and deployment conditions.

options.text: Upload the model to Cloud Storage and run predictions using Cloud Functions
options.explanation: The best option is to import the TensorFlow SavedModel into BigQuery ML and run predictions using SQL. This keeps the data in BigQuery and uses in-database compute to perform inference efficiently. Running predictions using Cloud Functions is not practical for large-scale batch jobs and would require orchestrating queries and handling input-output manually. Dataflow pipelines and Vertex AI batch prediction are both valid methods for batch inference, but they involve additional infrastructure and data transfer. questions.text: Your team has built a pipeline that cleans customer transaction data and stores the output in a Cloud Storage bucket. You want to trigger model retraining as soon as new data arrives. You use Kubeflow Pipelines on Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) for training, and you want to incorporate this into a CI/CD workflow that minimizes manual intervention and component complexity. What should you do?

options.text: Set up a Cloud Storage event trigger to launch a Cloud Run Function when a new file is added to the bucket. Use the Cloud Run Function to start the training job on a GKE cluster.
options.explanation:
The best solution is to use a Cloud Storage event trigger to launch a Cloud Run Function. Cloud Storage natively supports event notifications to Cloud Run Functions when objects are added to a bucket. This creates a lightweight, serverless, and event-driven workflow. The Cloud Run Function can then initiate the training job on your Kubeflow Pipelines setup running on GKE. This removes the need for intermediary services like Pub/Sub or polling mechanisms and integrates cleanly into a CI/CD setup for automated retraining.

The Dataflow approach adds unnecessary complexity and is not inherently event-driven. Polling via App Engine wastes resources and introduces latency. Cloud Scheduler introduces delay and adds complexity without offering real-time triggering. Direct Cloud Storage triggers are now the simplest and most efficient pattern for this use case.

questions.text: You are running a pipeline in Vertex AI that includes hyperparameter tuning using Bayesian search to optimize a regression model. The tuning stage is currently slowing down the overall workflow and is becoming a bottleneck. You want to reduce the tuning time while keeping the model performance acceptable. Which two changes would help you meet this goal?

options.text: Limit the floating-point values of parameters to a narrower range
options.explanation:
Narrowing the range of parameters reduces the size of the search space, helping the tuner find useful configurations faster. Enabling early stopping prevents the system from spending resources on trials that are clearly underperforming. These two adjustments reduce tuning time without meaningfully hurting final model quality.

Reducing the number of parallel trials would increase total tuning duration because fewer configurations are evaluated at once. Switching to random search makes the tuning process less efficient because it ignores the outcomes of earlier trials and often requires more iterations to find strong results.

questions.text: You are helping a hospital develop a model using AutoML to identify patients at risk for a rare complication following surgery. The complication is uncommon, but early detection is critical. You want to maximize the model’s ability to correctly detect these cases while limiting unnecessary alerts that could overwhelm clinical staff. Which training objective should you choose?

options.text: Use a training objective that maximizes the area under the precision-recall curve
options.explanation: Precision-recall is the right focus when dealing with rare events, such as a medical complication that happens infrequently. AUC PR emphasizes the model’s ability to correctly identify positive cases (recall) while limiting false alarms (precision). AUC ROC can be misleading when the class imbalance is severe because it includes true negative rate, which can be high just by predicting the dominant class. Log loss evaluates probability calibration but doesn’t directly prioritize positive class detection. Optimizing precision at a fixed recall can be helpful in some workflows, but it doesn't reflect the full tradeoff spectrum that AUC PR captures.

options.explanation: The first option evaluates model performance based on a clear and meaningful engagement metric. questions.text: You are building a TensorFlow-based neural network model to analyze customer transaction behavior for a retail analytics platform. The dataset includes features such as total amount spent, number of items per transaction, and customer tenure. During training, you observe that the model converges slowly and updates to the weights appear erratic. What preprocessing step should you prioritize?

options.text: Create a composite feature that multiplies transaction value and tenure to reflect customer lifetime value.
options.explanation: The correct approach is to normalize the features so they share a common range. When features differ widely in magnitude, gradient descent may overemphasize high-value inputs, which slows convergence and makes training unstable. This is especially relevant in neural networks, which rely on gradient-based updates. Creating new composite features might be helpful in some cases but does not address the inconsistent weight updates caused by uneven input scales. Removing rows with missing values may improve data quality but is not connected to the issue of gradients behaving poorly. Increasing the size of the training set can help improve generalization but does not fix convergence issues rooted in input feature scaling.

questions.text: A fintech company is developing fraud detection models using Vertex AI. The ML team runs hundreds of experiments varying data sampling methods, model types, and training parameters. What approach should they use to streamline their experimentation and minimize manual tracking?

options.text: Use Vertex AI Custom Training for each experiment. Write the evaluation metrics to BigQuery and access them using the BigQuery API.
options.explanation: The recommended approach is to use Vertex AI Pipelines along with Vertex AI Experiments. This combination enables the team to scale and automate experiment workflows while automatically tracking metrics in a structured format. The Vertex AI API provides direct access to the logged results, which supports streamlined analysis without needing to manually instrument tracking code. Writing metrics to BigQuery introduces extra effort and does not integrate directly with the Vertex AI experimentation framework. Logging to Cloud Logging is not designed for tracking ML metrics and does not store them in a way that supports structured querying. Using Workbench notebooks and Cloud Storage may allow for saving results, but lacks automation and requires additional effort to manage experiment versions and comparisons.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics firm is developing a classification model to predict rare adverse drug reactions based on electronic health records. The training dataset includes hundreds of thousands of patient records, but less than 2% contain documented adverse reactions. The team is using a tree-based ensemble method for modeling. What preprocessing strategy would most likely improve model performance?

options.text: Increase the number of adverse reaction examples by oversampling them in the training set.
options.explanation: The correct choice is to oversample the minority class, which in this case is the small set of records with adverse drug reactions. In highly imbalanced classification problems, especially with tree-based models, the model tends to favor the majority class and may learn very little from the rare but important cases. Oversampling helps rebalance the dataset and increases the likelihood that the model will learn meaningful patterns associated with the minority class. One-hot encoding and normalization can be useful in general, but they do not address the underlying imbalance. TFRecord formatting may improve performance during data ingestion but has no effect on how the model learns from imbalanced labels.
