questions.text: A news media company is building a system to process a collection of interview transcripts and automatically identify all references to people and organizations mentioned. The transcripts are stored as unstructured text files in Cloud Storage. The system must be trained to detect these mentions accurately across various writing styles. You need to choose a solution that minimizes labeling complexity while allowing you to build a domain-specific extraction model. What should you do?

options.text: Build a multi-class classification dataset in Vertex AI, label each transcript with all the people and organizations it contains, and train a classification model to predict those labels.
options.explanation:
The choice that uses Vertex AI Gemini Document Entity Extraction with a simple schema for person and organization best fits the problem. It treats the task as entity extraction rather than document classification, so the model learns to find exact text spans for names and organizations. Defining two broad entity types minimizes labeling effort while still covering all cases you care about. Tuning with a few hundred examples per type is usually enough to reach solid accuracy and can be improved over time. The workflow produces structured JSON that you can store or search across transcripts

Training a document classification model is wrong because classification assigns labels to an entire transcript and cannot identify where each mention occurs. Defining a separate label for every specific person and organization is wrong because it explodes the label space, increases annotation time, and fails to generalize to unseen names. The answer that uses the Natural Language API Entity Sentiment method is wrong because it is a generic model that is not tuned to your domain and sentiment is irrelevant to the requirement. It may miss names that appear in journalistic styles or informal speech, and you cannot easily adapt it with your own labeled examples.

questions.text: You work for a travel booking platform that provides hotel recommendations to users based on their recent search activity. Your current recommendation model is deployed on a Vertex AI endpoint. You have retrained the same model using the latest booking trends and want to evaluate its performance in production using real user traffic while making as few infrastructure changes as possible. You want to reduce the risk of negatively affecting user experience during testing. What should you do?

options.text: Deploy the new model to the same Vertex AI endpoint as the existing model. Use traffic splitting to send a small percentage of live user requests to the new model and gradually increase allocation based on engagement metrics
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to deploy the new model to the same Vertex AI endpoint and use traffic splitting. This approach allows you to run an A/B test in production without creating additional endpoints or building custom routing services. You can route a small portion of live user traffic to the new model, observe booking engagement and conversion metrics, and gradually ramp up if results are positive. This method minimizes operational changes while still giving a clear picture of how the new model performs under real-world conditions.

The other choices are less effective for this scenario. Setting up a second endpoint and using a Cloud Run Functions routing service works but adds unnecessary complexity when Vertex AI supports built-in traffic splitting. Running offline batch predictions against historical data does not capture real-time user behavior, which is critical for a recommendation system. Replacing the old model entirely and relying on Vertex AI Model Monitoring for feature drift detection does not compare performance between the two models and could expose all users to a potentially underperforming model without a safe rollback path.

options.text: Set up a second Vertex AI endpoint for the new model and implement a Cloud Run Functions service that sends a fixed percentage of user requests to that endpoint for evaluation. Adjust traffic routing manually based on performance trends over time

questions.text: You manage a data analytics workflow for a subscription video platform. A BigQuery ML logistic regression model predicts the likelihood that a subscriber will cancel in the next month. You want the model to retrain automatically every Monday using the latest engagement and viewing history data. Your goal is to keep the setup simple and the operational cost low. What should you do?

options.text: Configure a scheduled query in BigQuery to run the model retraining SQL each week
options.explanation:
The best approach is to use BigQuery’s built-in scheduling feature to run the retraining SQL automatically. BigQuery can execute a CREATE OR REPLACE MODEL statement on a recurring basis without additional infrastructure. This eliminates the need to manage separate scheduling services or orchestration tools and reduces both complexity and ongoing costs. For a straightforward recurring retrain task, this method is the most direct and least error-prone.

The other options introduce unnecessary overhead. A Vertex AI Pipeline with Cloud Scheduler is better suited for complex multi-step ML workflows, not a single SQL retrain. A Cloud Run Functions job triggered by Cloud Scheduler adds extra components that must be deployed and maintained. Using Workflows with Cloud Scheduler to call the BigQuery API also adds complexity without benefit for this case. All three alternatives require more development and operational effort than necessary for a simple, recurring BigQuery ML retraining task.

questions.text: You are building a machine learning model for a job-matching platform that recommends open positions to applicants. The dataset will include user activity from your production system. You want to ensure the model does not introduce or amplify bias against certain demographic groups. Which actions should you take? (Choose two.)

options.text: Exclude all demographic attributes from the dataset
options.explanation:
Stratified sampling on production data ensures that the training dataset both reflects the real-world inputs the model will see and includes adequate representation of underrepresented groups. Using production data captures the current patterns, behavior trends, and demographic distributions present in the live environment, which helps the model generalize effectively when deployed. The stratification step makes sure that groups that naturally appear less often in the production stream are still included in sufficient numbers for training. This combination reduces the risk of biased performance while keeping the dataset relevant to current use cases. Fairness testing across sensitive categories then provides a quantitative way to detect and address disparities in model predictions, which is an important step before deployment.

The other options are either incomplete or introduce bias. Simply excluding demographic attributes does not prevent bias if correlated features remain in the dataset. Limiting the data to groups with the highest activity skews the dataset toward majority groups, omitting others with valuable patterns. Using only data from previously hired applicants creates historical bias, as past hiring decisions may have been influenced by biased practices. These approaches fail to ensure balanced representation and fairness in the model.

Using only data from previously hired applicants creates historical bias, as past hiring decisions may have been influenced by biased practices. These approaches fail to ensure balanced representation and fairness in the model.

options.explanation:

The stratification step makes sure that groups that naturally appear less often in the production stream are still included in sufficient numbers for training. This combination reduces the risk of biased performance while keeping the dataset relevant to current use cases. questions.text: You are building a recommendation system in a Vertex AI Workbench notebook for an online bookstore. You want to log parameters, metrics, and artifacts for each experimental run so you can compare different algorithms. You also want to ensure that once you find a strong candidate model, you can quickly use it in a production pipeline without having to redo earlier work. How should you set up your workflow in Vertex AI?

options.text: Initialize the Vertex AI SDK with the experiment name, log parameters and metrics, and upload datasets to a Cloud Storage bucket while saving trained models to the Vertex AI Model Registry
options.explanation:
The correct answer uses the Vertex AI SDK to both log experiment metadata and explicitly attach datasets and models as artifacts to the execution. This keeps the datasets, models, parameters, and metrics connected in one place within Vertex AI Experiments. When artifacts are formally attached, Vertex AI can track them alongside each experiment run, making it easier to compare different approaches and seamlessly use the winning model in a production pipeline without having to locate or re-register the artifacts. This is the key difference between simply saving data in storage and formally attaching them as part of the experiment execution.

The other options are incorrect because they either omit the artifact attachment step or use tools in ways that do not integrate directly with Vertex AI Experiments. The first option logs metrics and uploads datasets and models, but it does not attach these artifacts to the execution, which breaks the link between experiments and the exact resources used. The third option uses Cloud Run Functions to log metrics and parameters, but this is unnecessary and complicates the workflow while bypassing built-in Vertex AI experiment tracking. The fourth option relies on AutoML and manual artifact handling, which does not establish the structured linkage between experiments and artifacts that is needed for rapid production transition.

questions.text: A retail analytics team is running experiments with Vertex AI Pipelines to process sales prediction models. They verified that running the pipeline from Cloud Shell works. They now want to trigger the pipeline from a Vertex AI Workbench user-managed notebook that is hosted in a separate project. After setting up the instance and running the code, the pipeline submission fails with a permissions error. What should they do?

options.text: Make sure the Workbench instance is in the same zone as the Vertex AI Pipelines resources
options.explanation:
The correct answer is to give the Compute Engine VM service account for the Workbench instance the IAM Vertex AI User role. Vertex AI requires this role to submit pipeline jobs. When using a user-managed notebook, the role must be granted to the instance’s service account in the relevant project so the notebook can execute the pipeline. Without this permission, even if the network and region are configured correctly, the submission will fail due to authorization checks performed by Vertex AI.

The other options are incorrect because they address configuration aspects unrelated to the IAM authorization error in this case. Placing the instance in the same zone or using the same VPC network may improve latency or meet network access requirements but will not fix missing permissions. Assigning a role intended for notebook execution management will allow the service account to run notebooks but will not give it authority to start Vertex AI Pipelines. These adjustments would leave the core IAM permission requirement unsolved.

questions.text: You work for a wildlife conservation organization building a system to detect endangered bird species from photos taken by motion-triggered cameras in remote forests. Each photo is uploaded to a Cloud Storage bucket along with metadata such as GPS coordinates, timestamp, and camera ID. You need to create a real-time solution to identify when an endangered bird is detected so field teams can respond quickly. You also want to ensure the solution achieves high accuracy by directly leveraging visual evidence from the images. What should you do?

options.text: Train an AutoML model on the metadata only, deploy the model to Vertex AI, and configure Pub/Sub to send an alert when predictions indicate a high likelihood of endangered species presence
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to train an AutoML image classification model on labeled bird photos so the model learns directly from visual features. Deploying the model to a Vertex AI endpoint allows for real-time classification of incoming images. Connecting the endpoint to Pub/Sub enables immediate alerts to be sent when the model detects an endangered species, ensuring timely field intervention. This workflow incorporates both accurate model training from labeled images and low-latency prediction for rapid decision-making.

The other options are less suitable for this need. Training only on metadata ignores the key visual cues that determine species identification and would likely reduce accuracy. Using K-means clustering in BigQuery ML is an unsupervised method that does not use labeled training data, so it cannot reliably distinguish endangered birds from other species. A daily batch prediction job delays the alerting process, making it unsuitable for real-time conservation actions that require quick response.

questions.text: A large-scale e-commerce platform maintains personalized search ranking models built with TensorFlow. The models use billions of clickstream events and over 80,000 unique categorical product IDs. The current training runs on a small GPU cluster, but as the dataset grows, training time has increased significantly. The team decides to migrate training to Google Cloud to improve scalability and performance for these models. You need to choose the most suitable approach for handling the large embedding tables efficiently while minimizing training time. What should you do?

options.text: Deploy the training jobs on Compute Engine VMs with multiple A100 GPUs and use standard TensorFlow embedding lookups
options.explanation:
TPU VMs with Pod slices are designed for high-throughput workloads like large-scale recommendation systems. The TPUEmbedding API is optimized for efficiently handling very large embedding tables, reducing communication overhead and making full use of TPU architecture. This combination accelerates training on datasets with billions of interactions and tens of thousands of categorical features, delivering significantly lower training time compared to CPU or even large GPU configurations. The v4 generation TPUs also provide improved scaling for embedding-heavy deep learning workloads.

The other options are not optimal for this use case. Running on A100 GPUs with standard embedding lookups can work for smaller embedding tables but is less efficient at scale due to memory bandwidth and lookup overhead. BigQuery ML does not support training deep learning recommendation models with large embedding workflows, and matrix factorization alone cannot capture the complex feature interactions present in this dataset. Using CPUs in an autoscaling GKE cluster does not provide enough computational throughput for large deep learning models, making training much slower compared to TPU-based approaches.

questions.text: You are building a fraud detection system for a financial services company. Your team has trained an XGBoost model to identify potentially fraudulent transactions. The model will be deployed on Vertex AI for online prediction, and you want each request to return results with minimal latency. You also want to be notified if the importance of certain features in the model changes significantly over time. You are uploading the trained model to Vertex AI Model Registry and must configure both the explanation method and monitoring strategy. What should you do?

options.text: Specify Integrated Gradients as the explanation method with a low path count, deploy the model to Vertex AI Endpoints, and create a Vertex AI Model Monitoring job that uses prediction drift as the monitoring objective
options.explanation:
Sampled Shapley is the correct choice for XGBoost models because Integrated Gradients is not supported for tree-based models. A low path count reduces computation and keeps latency low for online predictions, which matches the need for quick responses. Prediction drift monitoring is the right choice when you want to detect changes in model output patterns or feature attributions over time, ensuring alerts if important features begin to shift in influence. This approach balances the requirement for explainability, performance, and operational monitoring.

The other options are incorrect for specific reasons. Integrated Gradients is designed for differentiable models like neural networks, so it will not work with XGBoost. High path counts increase the computational cost of generating explanations, which leads to higher latency and violates the minimal-latency requirement. Training-serving skew monitoring checks for mismatches between training and serving data distributions but does not track changes in feature attribution over time, so it will not meet the stated monitoring goal. These limitations make the other configurations unsuitable for the requirements described.

questions.text: You work for a global e-commerce company that operates in multiple regions, including Europe and Australia. Your data science team has developed a TensorFlow model to predict product demand in real time. The model is deployed on Vertex AI in the europe-west4 region using n1-highmem-16 machines. You have received reports that users in Australia are experiencing slower response times compared to those in Europe. You want to reduce latency for Australian users while keeping the deployment architecture simple. What should you do?

options.text: Deploy the model to Vertex AI private endpoints in both europe-west4 and australia-southeast1, and configure your application to route to the correct endpoint
options.explanation:
The best approach is to deploy the model closer to Australian users. Creating a Vertex AI endpoint in australia-southeast1 ensures requests from that region avoid long network travel to Europe, which significantly reduces latency. Configuring the application to route traffic to the closest available endpoint allows users in each region to get faster responses without major changes to the overall architecture. This approach directly addresses the geographic latency issue.

The other options fail to solve the main problem. Private endpoints are for secure internal VPC connections and will not improve global latency. Adding GPUs in the Europe deployment will not reduce the network distance for Australian users and may even introduce unnecessary complexity. questions.text: A healthcare analytics company is building a custom training pipeline on Vertex AI to predict patient readmission risk. The training data is relatively small and stored in Cloud Storage. The training code uses scikit-learn and several other nonstandard Python packages that are not included in the default prebuilt containers. The data science team wants to minimize the startup time for each training job while making sure they can easily rerun the job multiple times during development. How should they configure the custom training job?

options.text: Use the scikit-learn prebuilt container, download the training data from Cloud Storage at runtime, and install the required dependencies using pip within the training script
options.explanation:
The correct solution is to create a custom container with all dependencies already included and store the dataset in Cloud Storage. This keeps the container lightweight while avoiding runtime dependency installation, which can significantly delay startup. Reading a small dataset from Cloud Storage has negligible impact on performance and allows data updates without rebuilding the container. This approach minimizes startup time, simplifies iteration, and keeps both data and code management flexible.

The other options introduce unnecessary delays or complexity. Installing dependencies at runtime slows job startup and can make results inconsistent if package versions change. Embedding the dataset inside the container increases image size, making it slower to pull and less flexible to update data without rebuilding the image. Using a prebuilt container without modification forces you to install dependencies during execution, which is slower than having them pre-installed. Storing data in Cloud Storage and pulling it at runtime is faster and more scalable than bundling it directly into the container for this scenario.

questions.text: A video analytics startup processes surveillance footage using an ML pipeline on Google Cloud. The pipeline runs multiple steps, including extracting frames, detecting objects, and storing structured metadata in BigQuery. Each step is already implemented as a separate service. You expect large batches of video files to arrive in a Cloud Storage bucket throughout the day. The orchestration should run only when new files arrive and should avoid idle compute costs. You also want the setup to scale automatically without constant polling. How should you design the orchestration layer?

options.text: Build the orchestration in Vertex AI Pipelines and configure a Cloud Run Functions trigger from Cloud Storage events to start the pipeline
options.explanation:
The best solution is to use Vertex AI Pipelines for orchestration and connect it to a Cloud Run Functions trigger that listens to Cloud Storage events. This ensures the pipeline runs only when triggered by a new file upload, eliminating idle processing and reducing unnecessary costs. The Cloud Run Function acts as a lightweight event-driven gateway, and Vertex AI Pipelines manages the sequencing of the ML workflow steps in a scalable, production-ready way.

The other options either increase costs or introduce unnecessary complexity. Triggering Cloud Composer from Cloud Run Functions still leaves you using a service better suited for general ETL workflows rather than ML pipelines, and it can incur overhead from maintaining Composer environments. Scheduling the pipeline at fixed intervals leads to wasted runs when no new files are available. Using file sensors in Cloud Composer requires persistent scheduling and monitoring, which contradicts the goal of avoiding idle compute and scaling purely on demand.

questions.text:
You are developing an end-to-end Vertex AI Pipelines workflow for a TensorFlow-based recommendation system. The pipeline retrieves product interaction data from BigQuery, processes it, applies several feature transformation steps, trains the model, evaluates its performance, and then uploads the model artifact to Cloud Storage.

You are testing multiple variations of the preprocessing and training components, but for most runs, only one or two steps change between executions. Each full run of the pipeline currently takes more than an hour, which is slowing down your development process. You want to shorten the execution time without increasing infrastructure costs.

What should you do?

options.text: Enable caching for all steps in the Vertex AI pipeline
options.explanation:
The correct answer is to enable step caching in the Vertex AI pipeline. This lets the pipeline reuse the output of steps whose inputs and code have not changed, instead of re-running them each time. When iterating on only certain parts of the workflow, this significantly reduces total execution time without increasing cost. Vertex AI Pipelines supports step caching natively, making it a straightforward optimization that fits well into iterative model development.

The other options are less suitable. Disabling steps breaks the defined pipeline structure and risks dependency errors. Moving transformations to BigQuery can shift the workload but will not meaningfully shorten overall runtime unless that stage is the main bottleneck. Adding GPUs increases cost and only helps in compute-heavy stages, which does not match the requirement to avoid extra cost.

options.text: Temporarily disable steps in the pipeline that are not being modified during testing

questions.text:
A financial services company is building a fraud detection system on Google Cloud. Transaction records from multiple regional systems are exported daily to a Cloud Storage bucket in a mix of Parquet and Avro formats. You need to design a data pipeline that will run analytics, generate machine learning features, and store those features so deployed models can access them with low-latency for online predictions.

What should you do?

options.text: Ingest the Parquet and Avro files into BigQuery for analytics, transform the results into ML features with Dataflow, and store the features in Vertex AI Feature Store for online prediction
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to use BigQuery to ingest and analyze the Parquet and Avro data, then use Dataflow to process and transform the output into machine learning features, and finally store those features in Vertex AI Feature Store for serving. BigQuery natively supports both Parquet and Avro formats and can run large-scale analytical queries efficiently. Dataflow integrates well with BigQuery outputs and can perform the necessary feature transformations before sending them to Vertex AI Feature Store, which is built for low-latency, versioned feature serving. This architecture meets both the analytical and online prediction requirements.

The other choices fall short for various reasons. Using BigQuery to produce features and keeping them in standard BigQuery tables does not deliver the low-latency serving or feature management capabilities that a feature store provides. Cloud SQL is not intended for large-scale analytics and would be inefficient for processing large Parquet or Avro datasets. Cloud Spanner is optimized for globally distributed transactions, not heavy analytics workloads, and storing features in BigQuery tables instead of a feature store would fail to meet performance and versioning needs for real-time model predictions.

questions.text:
A law firm stores client deposition recordings in Cloud Storage for later review and analysis. The depositions are recorded using VoIP equipment at a 12 kHz sample rate. Most recordings last between 4 and 8 minutes. The legal team needs a transcription process that can handle these files reliably so they can be searched and referenced in case preparation. You need to follow Google Cloud’s best practices when setting up the transcription workflow.

What should you do?

options.text: Upsample the recordings to 24 kHz and use the Speech-to-Text API with long-running transcription requests
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to keep the original 12 kHz recordings and use asynchronous transcription. The Speech-to-Text API can process 12 kHz audio directly, so there is no need to upsample. Since the recordings are several minutes long, asynchronous transcription is the appropriate method, as it can handle longer files without running into the time limits or reliability issues that synchronous transcription can face. This setup ensures the depositions can be transcribed accurately without unnecessary audio processing.

The other choices have clear drawbacks. Upsampling to 24 kHz adds extra processing time without improving transcription quality and can introduce distortion. Synchronous transcription on multi-minute recordings risks timeouts or partial transcripts. Combining upsampling with synchronous transcription not only adds unnecessary processing but also uses an unsuitable transcription method for the length of the recordings, making it the least effective choice.

questions.text: A nonprofit organization is expanding into multiple countries in Latin America. Staff members regularly receive Portuguese-language reports from partners in Brazil and need to quickly produce English versions for internal review. The reports are long and vary in formatting, including tables and embedded charts. The organization does not require domain-specific terminology or special customization, and the translation process should require minimal technical setup. You are responsible for recommending a Google Cloud solution to meet this requirement.

options.text: Use the Cloud Translation API to translate individual sentences extracted from the reports after converting them into plain text using a custom Python script in a Cloud Run Functions service
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to use the Document Translation feature of the Cloud Translation API. This service can process complete documents, preserving formatting, and translating them directly without requiring text extraction or manual segmentation. It is designed for general-purpose translations when domain-specific customization is not needed and has minimal setup requirements. This approach suits the nonprofit’s need for quick, low-effort translations that handle long, formatted documents.

Using a custom Cloud Run Functions script to extract sentences adds unnecessary engineering work and loses original formatting. Translating manually in Google Sheets is inefficient and prone to errors for long documents. Training an AutoML model with a small dataset will result in lower translation quality than using Google’s pretrained model, and it requires far more setup effort. None of these options meets the requirement for minimal configuration while handling full document formats.

They need to determine the best combination of learning rate, dropout rate, and convolution filter sizes. The team wants to test many parameter combinations in parallel and avoid building custom orchestration logic or infrastructure to run these experiments. They also want to minimize manual coding for tuning logic.

What should they do?

options.text: Create a training workflow that manually submits multiple training jobs to Vertex AI using a custom Python script to vary parameters
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to create a Vertex AI hyperparameter tuning job. This feature automates the search across defined parameter ranges, running multiple trials in parallel without requiring the team to handle job orchestration or optimization code. It allows them to explore different learning rates, dropout values, and filter sizes efficiently while keeping the tuning process fully managed within Vertex AI. This approach fits the need for parallel experimentation with minimal custom code.

The other choices introduce more manual effort or do not support tuning the custom model as needed. Writing a Python script to launch jobs handles parallelism manually but lacks the automated optimization capabilities of a tuning job. AutoML will automatically select a model and parameters, but it does not allow tuning of the team’s existing custom architecture. questions.text:
A wildlife conservation group operates drones to monitor animal populations across multiple nature reserves. Each drone captures images every few minutes and uploads them to a Cloud Storage bucket. Currently, volunteers manually scan the images to locate and tag animals before recording them in a database. The group wants to reduce the time spent on tagging while keeping upfront costs low and avoiding model training. They need an approach that scales easily and requires minimal setup.

What should they do?

options.text: Build a custom object detection model using Vertex AI with a dataset of animal images and deploy it for tagging
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to use the Cloud Vision API for object detection and labeling. This service is pre-trained to recognize many common objects, can be called directly without model training, and integrates easily with Cloud Storage workflows. It is well suited for situations where rapid deployment and minimal upfront cost are important, and where a general-purpose model meets the accuracy needs. The group can quickly add this step before the database ingestion process so volunteers focus on verification instead of tagging from scratch.

The other options require more work and resources than necessary for the requirements. A custom Vertex AI model needs labeled training data, infrastructure setup, and ongoing retraining, which adds cost and complexity. BigQuery ML does not support object detection for images and cannot perform the tagging task directly. An open-source detection framework deployed on Vertex AI would still require building, training, and maintaining the model, which conflicts with the need for a quick and low-effort solution.

questions.text:
A retail analytics company needs to automate a workflow that regularly retrains gradient boosting models using new sales data from multiple regions. The process involves steps for extracting data from BigQuery, performing distributed preprocessing with PySpark, training models, validating performance, and pushing them to an endpoint for predictions. The team wants a managed orchestration service that integrates directly with Dataproc for PySpark tasks and reduces infrastructure management overhead.

What should they do?

options.text: Build a machine learning workflow in Kubeflow Pipelines on Google Kubernetes Engine and create a custom component to run the PySpark preprocessing on Dataproc
options.explanation:
The best choice is to use Vertex AI Pipelines with the built-in Dataproc component for the PySpark step. This service is fully managed and works well for end-to-end ML workflows while minimizing infrastructure overhead. The native Dataproc integration means the PySpark preprocessing step can be included without writing custom orchestration code, and the pipeline can coordinate other tasks such as training and deployment in the same environment. This makes it easier to maintain and scale without having to manage Kubernetes clusters or build a custom orchestration layer.

The other choices all introduce unnecessary complexity for this use case. Kubeflow Pipelines on GKE adds operational overhead because the Kubernetes infrastructure must be managed and updated. Cloud Composer is a general-purpose orchestrator and does not provide the same level of ML-specific integration as Vertex AI Pipelines. TFX pipelines are intended for TensorFlow workloads, and adapting them for PySpark preprocessing adds complexity without delivering additional benefits here.

questions.text:
A subscription-based music platform uses an AutoML classification model to recommend playlists to users based on listening history. You are deploying the model to a new Vertex AI endpoint that will integrate with the platform’s mobile app. The service experiences significantly more prediction requests during weekday evenings and on Friday nights. You must configure the model deployment to keep latency low during busy periods while avoiding unnecessary cost during slower hours.

What should you do?

options.text: Configure the endpoint to use an n1-standard-4 machine type with a minReplicaCount of 1 and a maxReplicaCount of 8
options.explanation:
The best approach is to choose an n1-standard-4 machine type with autoscaling from 1 to 8 replicas. This machine type is appropriate for serving AutoML models without overprovisioning. Setting the minimum replica count to 1 ensures that the service remains available during low-traffic periods while keeping costs down. Allowing the maximum replica count to scale to 8 handles peak demand during evening hours and on Fridays, keeping latency low without paying for unused resources during off-peak times.

The other options are less efficient or incompatible with the needs of the service. An n1-standard-32 machine type is unnecessarily large for AutoML model serving and will increase costs. For most AutoML models, GPUs don’t help with deployment, except potentially for AutoML image models where inference can be GPU-accelerated, so adding a GPU here would increase cost without benefit. Setting a higher minimum replica count, such as 4, increases baseline costs even during quiet periods without improving peak performance, since autoscaling already manages additional capacity as needed.

questions.text:
An online real estate analytics company trains a BigQuery ML logistic regression model to predict the likelihood that a property listing will sell within 60 days. The training data is stored in BigQuery, and new listings are continuously added throughout the day. The preprocessing for the model includes bucketizing the square footage feature and normalizing the listing price feature using statistics computed on the most recent data. You want to keep the model retraining process simple while avoiding unnecessary storage and extra query costs.

What should you do?

options.text: Stage the processed listing data in a new BigQuery table using scheduled SQL queries before retraining the model
options.explanation:
The most efficient solution is to use the TRANSFORM clause in the CREATE MODEL statement. This lets you perform bucketization and normalization inline during model creation without having to stage intermediate results or store calculated statistics separately. Doing preprocessing at this step means only the required data is processed at the time of model training, which keeps both storage usage and query cost low while reducing the complexity of the workflow.

The other options add unnecessary steps or storage overhead. Staging processed data in a new table increases storage costs and introduces the need to manage refresh logic. Building preprocessing into Vertex AI Pipelines with intermediate tables adds complexity and maintenance without improving performance in this case. Storing statistics in a separate dataset and joining them into the training query increases storage use and query cost unnecessarily when preprocessing can be embedded directly in the CREATE MODEL statement.

questions.text:
A financial analytics startup built a custom training script in PyTorch that can train either a decision tree regressor or a gradient boosting model, controlled by a model_type parameter. When model_type is set to gradient_boosting, additional parameters such as max_depth and learning_rate are used. You plan to use Vertex AI hyperparameter tuning with a trial budget of 120 runs. Your goal is to find the combination of model_type and associated hyperparameters that results in the best predictive accuracy.

What should you do?

options.text: Launch separate hyperparameter tuning jobs for each model_type, splitting the budget evenly, then compare their final results
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to run one hyperparameter tuning job and make max_depth and learning_rate conditional on the parent hyperparameter model_type. This ensures that these two parameters are only considered when the gradient boosting model is being tested. By structuring the search space this way, Vertex AI focuses trials on valid combinations and avoids wasting resources testing irrelevant hyperparameters for the decision tree model. This method also allows both model types to be evaluated under the same tuning process, giving the optimizer the flexibility to find the best architecture and parameter settings within the same budget.

The other options create inefficiencies. Running separate jobs splits the budget, which limits exploration for each architecture. Running an initial job on model_type alone and then tuning only the selected one risks missing better combinations that could have emerged if tuning was done jointly. Making max_depth conditional but leaving learning_rate non-conditional wastes trials because learning_rate would still be evaluated for decision tree runs where it has no effect.

Making max_depth conditional but leaving learning_rate non-conditional wastes trials because learning_rate would still be evaluated for decision tree runs where it has no effect.

questions.text:
A renewable energy company built a Vertex AI AutoML model to predict the next day’s solar farm output using weather forecasts, historical production data, and maintenance schedules. The model is deployed to production and receives prediction requests daily. Data availability varies depending on weather station reporting, so some days provide fewer records than others. The team is concerned that shifts in seasonal sunlight patterns and changes in weather prediction accuracy could alter how features contribute to predictions. They want to detect these changes reliably and are not constrained by monitoring costs.

What should you do?

options.text: Create a feature drift monitoring job with a sampling rate of 0.1 and a weekly monitoring frequency
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to use feature attribution drift monitoring with a sampling rate higher than 0.5 and a weekly frequency. A higher sampling rate ensures that most of the available data is included in the monitoring process, which is important when incoming records may be sparse on certain days. This setup focuses on detecting shifts in how features influence predictions, such as changes in the relationship between forecasted cloud cover and actual solar output. Weekly monitoring provides timely detection while still smoothing out short-term fluctuations that could appear in sparse datasets.

The other options are less effective. Feature drift monitoring only identifies distribution changes, not shifts in predictive contribution, which is the key concern here. Monthly monitoring delays detection too much for operational decision-making. A sampling rate of 0.5 or lower risks missing important changes in attribution patterns, especially when the dataset for a given period is small. Lower sampling rates are more relevant when controlling costs, which is not a constraint in this scenario.

questions.text:
A media analytics company is building a TensorFlow Extended (TFX) pipeline to process and model clickstream data from multiple news sites. The pipeline includes several preprocessing steps, such as sessionization and normalization, before model training. Once in production, the pipeline will handle over 80 TB of BigQuery data each day. The team needs to run preprocessing at scale, store artifacts for reproducibility, and log experiment metrics to Vertex AI Experiments while keeping orchestration fully managed.

What should you do?

options.text: Run the TFX pipeline in Dataflow directly and configure Apache Beam to write logs to Vertex AI Experiments
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to run the TFX pipeline in Vertex AI Pipelines and set Apache Beam parameters so the preprocessing steps run in Dataflow. This leverages Vertex AI Pipelines for managed orchestration while ensuring large-scale distributed data processing is handled by Dataflow, which integrates cleanly with Apache Beam. It also enables direct tracking of artifacts and parameters in Vertex ML Metadata and logging of metrics in Vertex AI Experiments without adding orchestration complexity. This setup is well suited for processing tens of terabytes daily with high scalability.

The other options either remove the benefits of Vertex AI Pipelines or misuse services. Running Dataflow directly bypasses managed orchestration and reduces integration with Vertex AI metadata tracking. Using a Vertex AI Training custom job for preprocessing is inefficient since it is not designed for Apache Beam-based ETL workloads. Running on Dataproc with manual metric exports adds operational overhead and misses the native integration features between Vertex AI Pipelines and its metadata and experiment tracking systems.

questions.text:
A retail analytics company needs to train a custom demand forecasting model and then run large-scale batch predictions for upcoming seasonal sales. The team must track the complete lineage of both the trained model and the prediction outputs to meet compliance requirements. All lineage tracking should be automated, and the workflow should integrate with Vertex ML Metadata.

What should you do?

options.text: Create a Vertex AI Pipelines custom training job component to train the model and a Vertex AI Pipelines batch prediction component to generate forecasts
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to run both the training and batch prediction as components within Vertex AI Pipelines. This ensures all datasets, parameters, models, and prediction results are automatically tracked in Vertex ML Metadata. By having both steps in the same pipeline, the lineage from raw data through to predictions is complete and auditable without manual intervention, which satisfies compliance and operational traceability requirements.

The other options break the automated lineage tracking chain or use services that do not integrate fully with Vertex ML Metadata in this way. Training in a custom job and calling predictions via Cloud Run Functions does not create end-to-end metadata tracking. Using Model Registry with ad-hoc batch prediction jobs only tracks model versions, not the full workflow from data to predictions. Running part of the process in BigQuery ML after training elsewhere splits the workflow into disconnected systems, losing consistent lineage tracking.

options.text: Train the model in a Vertex AI custom training job and generate forecasts with a Cloud Run Functions service that calls the Vertex AI Prediction API

questions.text:
A regional airline keeps detailed flight, booking, and customer data in BigQuery. The business team wants a predictive model that estimates the probability of a customer booking another flight in the next six months. The solution should require no extra infrastructure setup and should be implemented entirely in SQL for ease of use.

What should you do?

options.text: Create a Vertex AI Workbench notebook and use Python code to call the BigQuery ML API for logistic regression
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to use BigQuery’s CREATE MODEL statement to build an AutoML classification model directly in the Google Cloud console. This approach works fully within SQL, requires no external environment, and lets BigQuery handle feature selection, algorithm choice, and hyperparameter tuning. Since the data already resides in BigQuery, the model can be trained without any additional data movement or infrastructure setup.

The other options either add unnecessary complexity or are not the right fit for the task. Using Vertex AI Workbench for logistic regression or ARIMA modeling requires managing a separate environment and code, which goes against the requirement for a SQL-only solution. ARIMA models are intended for time series forecasting, not classification problems like predicting customer booking likelihood. Creating a logistic regression model in BigQuery with fixed hyperparameters is a valid approach but requires more manual tuning and lacks AutoML’s automated optimization, making it less aligned with the simplicity requirement.

questions.text:
You work for a subscription-based streaming service and need to develop a BigQuery ML model that predicts the number of new subscribers for each region each month. Your dataset in BigQuery includes columns such as signup date, region, subscription plan, and promotional campaign ID. You want to choose the combination of model type and feature engineering approach that will best capture important patterns in the data while keeping the workflow straightforward.

What should you do?

options.text: Use a linear regression model. One-hot encode categorical features and extract relevant components from the date, such as month number and day of week
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to use linear regression in BigQuery ML with one-hot encoding for categorical variables and derived features from the date column. One-hot encoding preserves the categorical nature of the variables without introducing false ordinal relationships. Extracting elements like month and day of week provides the model with cyclical and seasonal patterns that often influence subscriber counts. This combination is simple to implement in BigQuery ML and aligns with the goal of capturing meaningful patterns without overcomplicating the workflow.

The other options have clear drawbacks for this task. K-means clustering is for unsupervised learning and would not generate predictions for a numeric target like subscriber counts. A deep neural network with embeddings can handle categorical data but introduces unnecessary complexity and tuning requirements for this scale of problem. ARIMA is intended for time series forecasting on a single metric over time, not for incorporating multiple categorical predictors in a regression context. Label encoding in both the ARIMA and k-means options risks imposing artificial order on categories, which can mislead these models.

questions.text:
Your company’s IT help desk team wants to launch an internal virtual assistant for employees to troubleshoot common technical issues. The assistant must answer questions using information from troubleshooting guides stored as Word documents, PDF manuals, and HTML help pages. You want to create this assistant quickly and avoid building or managing infrastructure.

What should you do?

options.text: Use Vertex AI Agent Builder to create an assistant. Securely index the troubleshooting guides into the assistant’s datastore and send user queries to return grounded responses
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to use Vertex AI Agent Builder to create the assistant and index the troubleshooting content directly into its datastore. This allows the assistant to provide grounded responses by retrieving relevant sections from the indexed content, avoiding the need to fine-tune large models or manage infrastructure. It is a fast, low-maintenance way to support interactive, semantic query answering across multiple file formats.

The other approaches introduce unnecessary complexity or fail to meet requirements. Fine-tuning a Gemini model on documents would be time-intensive, resource-heavy, and increase ongoing maintenance. A custom keyword-based search portal on Compute Engine would return less relevant answers since it lacks semantic understanding. An AppSheet app could provide browsing capability but would not handle natural language queries or dynamically retrieve the most relevant document excerpts.

questions.text:
A real estate company wants to automatically tag property listing images with attributes such as “kitchen,” “garden,” or “swimming pool” to make property searches more relevant for customers. They have a large dataset of labeled property images covering these categories. The solution must handle model training, tuning, and deployment without requiring the team to build ML infrastructure from scratch.

What should you do?

options.text: Build an image tagging system using a series of image processing rules and filters to detect specific objects in the photos
options.explanation:
The best approach is to use AutoML image classification to train the model directly on the labeled property images. AutoML image classification manages the preprocessing, training, and hyperparameter tuning without requiring deep ML expertise or building infrastructure. It also makes deployment straightforward, allowing the business to quickly integrate the model into their property search system.

The other options introduce issues that make them less suitable. A fully custom TensorFlow pipeline would require substantial ML expertise and manual optimization, which increases time and complexity. A rules-based approach is not robust for complex or varied image content and would fail to generalize well. Using a pre-trained model from Model Garden may seem fast, but such models are often built for broad categories and would need significant fine-tuning to accurately detect specialized property attributes, which adds extra work and resources.

options.text: Create a custom computer vision pipeline in Vertex AI using TensorFlow, train the model with the labeled dataset, and manually tune hyperparameters before deployment
options.explanation:
The best approach is to use AutoML image classification to train the model directly on the labeled property images. AutoML image classification manages the preprocessing, training, and hyperparameter tuning without requiring deep ML expertise or building infrastructure. It also makes deployment straightforward, allowing the business to quickly integrate the model into their property search system.

The other options introduce issues that make them less suitable. A fully custom TensorFlow pipeline would require substantial ML expertise and manual optimization, which increases time and complexity. A rules-based approach is not robust for complex or varied image content and would fail to generalize well. Using a pre-trained model from Model Garden may seem fast, but such models are often built for broad categories and would need significant fine-tuning to accurately detect specialized property attributes, which adds extra work and resources.

questions.text:
A financial services company is building a voice-based assistant that helps customers with their account inquiries. Voice transcriptions will be stored for quality assurance and analytics. Before storing this data, the company must ensure that any sensitive customer details, such as account numbers or addresses, are removed or replaced so they cannot be used to identify individuals.

What should you do?

options.text: Use the Cloud Speech-to-Text API to transcribe calls and configure it to automatically remove sensitive phrases before output
options.explanation:
The DLP API is designed to detect and transform sensitive information in text, images, and other data sources. In this case, it can scan the transcribed conversations, identify sensitive elements like account numbers, and then mask or tokenize them so that no identifiable information is stored. This approach ensures compliance with privacy requirements and reduces the risk of exposing sensitive customer data while still allowing the processed text to be used for analysis.

The other options have limitations that make them less appropriate here. Using Speech-to-Text with simple phrase filtering would be less reliable because it does not match the breadth or accuracy of the DLP API’s pattern detection. Entity extraction from Cloud Natural Language is not intended for PII detection or anonymization, so storing those entities separately would still risk leaving sensitive data in the original transcripts. Encrypting all customer data via DLP before storage is not the right use of the API in this context, as the goal is to remove or transform sensitive fields rather than encrypt the entire dataset, which would make it unusable for further analysis without decryption.

questions.text:
A media analytics company is building multiple custom models to automatically score video transcripts for sentiment and engagement. You have generated batch inference results from each model version and stored the outputs in Cloud Storage. You want to integrate an evaluation stage into your existing Vertex AI pipeline so that each run automatically calculates metrics, stores them in BigQuery, and keeps track of all artifacts for reproducibility.

What should you do?

options.text: Create a custom Vertex AI Pipelines component that processes the outputs from Cloud Storage, computes evaluation metrics, and writes the results to BigQuery
options.explanation:
A custom Vertex AI Pipelines component is the best choice here because it integrates directly into your existing pipeline, ensuring that metric calculations and artifact tracking are managed in a consistent, repeatable way. It allows you to store evaluation outputs in BigQuery while also preserving pipeline metadata in Vertex AI, which supports reproducibility and traceability for each model version tested. This keeps all workflow steps in one orchestrated environment without relying on disconnected tools.

The other options either fail to integrate with Vertex AI Pipelines or do not address the evaluation requirement in the intended way. A standalone Python script can calculate metrics but will not automatically track artifacts or integrate with the pipeline’s execution history. A Dataflow job can perform distributed computation, but here the workload does not require distributed processing, so it would add unnecessary complexity. Vertex AI Model Monitoring is meant for tracking model performance on live prediction data, not for offline evaluation of multiple model versions using batch inference results.

questions.text: A logistics company stores unstructured driver delivery logs in Cloud Storage. These logs contain both operational metrics and customer PII (Personally Identifiable Information) such as names, phone numbers, and addresses. You plan to process the logs into a structured format for downstream analytics in BigQuery. Company policy mandates that personally identifiable information must not leave Cloud Storage in identifiable form. You need a scalable approach that keeps the analytics data useful while meeting the policy requirements. What should you do?

options.text: Apply BigQuery row-level security after loading the data to control access to the PII
options.explanation:
The DLP API can detect and de-identify personally identifiable information in files stored in Cloud Storage before they are moved to other systems. De-identification methods such as masking, tokenization, or generalization ensure the PII is no longer directly linked to individuals while preserving the analytical value of the data. This allows you to transform the unstructured logs into structured form and load them into BigQuery without violating the policy on storing identifiable PII.

The other options fail to satisfy the requirement or introduce avoidable complexity. Row-level security in BigQuery controls access but does not remove the PII, so the data would still exist in identifiable form in BigQuery. Storing the raw logs in a restricted dataset still moves identifiable PII out of Cloud Storage, which breaks policy. Writing a Cloud Run Functions service to manually remove PII is less scalable, harder to maintain, and more error-prone than using the built-in detection and de-identification features of the DLP API.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company deploys machine learning models on Vertex AI to predict patient readmission risk. Hospital clients have requested per-prediction transparency that shows which features most influenced each result. Some models were built with AutoML and others are custom-trained. You need one consistent approach that integrates with your existing deployment on Vertex AI and requires minimal ongoing maintenance. What should you do?

options.text: Build a custom SHAP explainer running on Cloud Run Functions that processes each prediction request and returns the results to clients
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to enable Vertex Explainable AI for the deployed models. This service supports both AutoML and custom models and integrates directly into the existing prediction flow. When enabled, it produces feature-based explanations with each prediction, allowing clients to see which inputs contributed most to a particular result. This approach avoids building and maintaining separate infrastructure for explainability and meets the requirement for consistent, low-maintenance integration across multiple model types.

The other options have limitations. Running a SHAP explainer on Cloud Run Functions would require significant custom engineering, version control, and operational upkeep. Calculating feature importance in BigQuery as a daily batch process would only provide aggregate-level insights, not per-prediction detail. Vertex AI Model Monitoring focuses on detecting data drift and skew, not generating explanations for individual predictions, so it does not meet the stated requirements.

questions.text: A logistics company receives thousands of shipping manifests each day in PDF format. They need to extract details such as shipment ID, delivery address, and weight from these manifests and make the data available for analytics in BigQuery. The solution should require minimal custom development and no custom model training. What should you do?

options.text: Use the Cloud Document AI API to process the shipping manifests and extract the required fields
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to use the Cloud Document AI API. This service is designed for structured and semi-structured document processing and comes with pre-trained models capable of extracting key fields from shipping manifests without the need for building or training your own model. It outputs structured data directly, which can then be loaded into BigQuery with minimal additional processing. This approach meets the requirement for minimal custom code while avoiding the complexity of building and maintaining a custom extraction workflow.

The other options are less suitable. Using the Vision API with DOCUMENT_TEXT_DETECTION would only provide raw text, requiring significant additional parsing logic to identify specific fields. A custom-trained model in Vertex AI would require labeled training data and ongoing model lifecycle management, which conflicts with the goal of minimal development. AutoML could extract entities but would still require a training dataset and model management, making it more work than necessary when a specialized, pre-trained API already exists for the task.

questions.text: A travel company operates a website where customers can search for vacation packages. The current search tool only matches keywords in package titles and descriptions. Recently, more users have started entering conversational queries that include specific attributes like destination, travel dates, and activities. You need to quickly build a proof-of-concept search tool that can interpret these types of queries and return relevant packages without requiring you to train and deploy your own model. How should you build this search tool?

options.text: Create the search tool with Vertex AI Agent Builder
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to use Vertex AI Agent Builder. This service enables rapid creation of applications that can understand and respond to complex, natural language queries over structured datasets. It does not require you to train your own model or implement backend retrieval logic, which makes it well-suited for quickly delivering a proof-of-concept under time constraints. It can integrate with your structured vacation package data and interpret conversational queries that include specific attributes like location or activities.

The other options all require more setup and ongoing management. Vertex AI Vector Search would need you to build or source an embedding model, create and maintain a vector index, and implement custom retrieval logic. Hosting a BERT-based model on a Vertex AI endpoint demands fine-tuning, deployment work, and ongoing model management. Using a foundation model from Model Garden would require building a prompt-handling and retrieval backend along with possible tuning for your dataset, adding unnecessary complexity compared to a purpose-built tool for structured data search.

questions.text: You are a data scientist for an e-commerce retailer. You developed a TensorFlow model that predicts which products should be restocked every two weeks based on historical sales and inventory levels. The supply chain team wants to run this prediction process on a fixed biweekly schedule so that procurement orders can be generated in bulk. You need to deploy the model in a way that minimizes infrastructure management and ongoing maintenance. What should you do?

options.text: Deploy the model using a custom container on Cloud Run Functions and configure it to return predictions when triggered by an HTTP request biweekly
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to deploy the model to a Vertex AI endpoint for batch prediction. This is well suited to fixed, periodic schedules such as every two weeks, and it allows processing in bulk. Vertex AI’s managed batch prediction eliminates the need for additional infrastructure, and tools like Cloud Scheduler or Workflows can easily trigger it on a biweekly cadence. This setup ensures minimal maintenance while allowing the team to process all necessary data at one time.

The other options are less appropriate for this situation. Using Cloud Run Functions with online prediction is intended for near real-time requests and adds unnecessary complexity for bulk predictions on a fixed schedule. Running online prediction via a biweekly Cloud Scheduler job still keeps the model deployed for low-latency serving rather than efficient batch handling. Deploying to GKE would require more infrastructure management and manual setup for the biweekly processing workflow, which increases operational overhead compared to Vertex AI’s built-in batch prediction feature.

questions.text: A financial analytics firm is training a deep learning model in TensorFlow Keras to detect anomalies in large-scale transaction data. The training run is expected to last several days and requires high-precision float32 arithmetic. The model is built entirely with built-in TensorFlow operations. The engineering team wants to update the code to use tf.distribute.Strategy for distributed training and run the job on Compute Engine to reduce total training time. You need to configure the training environment to allow horizontal scaling while supporting efficient data distribution. What should you do?

options.text: Create an instance group with GPU-enabled Compute Engine instances, scale up the machine type until performance plateaus, add TF_CONFIG and MultiWorkerMirroredStrategy to the code, and configure data autosharding
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to use an instance group with GPU-enabled machines and MultiWorkerMirroredStrategy, configured with TF_CONFIG for distributed execution and data autosharding. This setup supports high-precision float32 workloads while allowing horizontal scaling across multiple workers. By starting with a smaller machine type and scaling until gains level off, you can find the optimal balance between compute resources and execution time. MultiWorkerMirroredStrategy provides synchronous training across multiple GPU-equipped instances, and data autosharding helps distribute the dataset efficiently to each worker.

The other options are less appropriate. A single-machine MirroredStrategy limits scaling potential since it only uses GPUs on one instance, which can become a bottleneck for multi-day training. TPU-based options are not well suited here because TPUs are optimized for lower-precision formats like bfloat16 and may not deliver the same efficiency for float32 workloads. Both TPU choices also require additional initialization and configuration steps that do not align with the needs of a high-precision model built exclusively with TensorFlow operations for GPU execution.

questions.text: Your company’s fraud detection system uses a gradient boosting model trained on customer transaction logs. The model has been running in production for 9 months. A new compliance policy now requires that any features containing personally identifiable information (PII) must be de-identified before being processed, and the data team has identified these features using the Cloud Data Loss Prevention API. You need to modify the data pipeline to comply with the policy while minimizing the loss of predictive accuracy. What should you do?

options.text: Replace the PII-containing features with null values, and retrain the model from scratch
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to use key-based hashing to transform the PII-containing features and then retrain the model from scratch. Hashing ensures that the transformation is irreversible, which satisfies compliance requirements for anonymization, while still producing consistent outputs for identical inputs. This preserves feature relationships and referential integrity in the dataset, allowing the model to maintain similar predictive patterns even without direct access to the original PII. Retraining from scratch avoids the risk of the previous model having memorized sensitive information and ensures that all learned patterns are based on the anonymized data.

The other approaches have shortcomings. Nulling out features would remove potentially important information, which could significantly reduce predictive accuracy. Randomized masking breaks consistency between records, preventing the model from recognizing repeat entities and hurting performance. Reversible encryption is not compliant with many anonymization regulations because the data can be restored to its original form, and resuming from a checkpoint risks retaining sensitive information learned before anonymization.

questions.text: You are working for a construction safety company that wants to automatically detect whether workers are wearing safety helmets in images captured from job site cameras. The helmets may appear in different shapes, colors, and sizes depending on the angle and lighting conditions. You have a dataset of 1,200 labeled images stored in Cloud Storage, along with a CSV annotation file. You need to choose a training method that can detect helmets at varying relative sizes in the images while keeping model development time as short as possible. What should you do?

options.text: Create a custom TensorFlow training script for object detection and package it for a custom training job in Vertex AI
options.explanation:
The best approach is to upload the dataset into Vertex AI and use AutoML Vision Image Object Detection with accuracy as the optimization metric, early stopping enabled, and no training budget specified. This method supports object detection for items that can vary in size, shape, and position within images, and the codeless training setup minimizes development time. AutoML Vision Image Object Detection automatically handles model architecture search, transfer learning, and hyperparameter optimization tailored to the provided dataset, which is particularly important when detection targets can appear in different scales.

Other approaches are less suited to the requirements here. Using the Cloud Vision API with OBJECT_LOCALIZATION type would limit flexibility and may struggle with reliably detecting helmets at a wide range of sizes. Building a custom TensorFlow object detection model or adapting a pre-trained image classification model for detection would require significant manual development, experimentation, and tuning, increasing the time needed to deliver a working solution. These approaches also demand more ML engineering expertise without guaranteeing better results compared to AutoML Vision Image Object Detection for this dataset size and task.

questions.text:
You have been developing a machine learning model in Vertex AI Workbench that predicts delivery delays for a logistics company. Your notebook follows these steps:

  • Load shipment data from BigQuery
  • Use TensorFlow Transform for feature scaling and encoding
  • Define a sequential Keras model using TensorFlow operators
  • Train and evaluate the model using model.fit() in the notebook environment
  • Save the trained model to Cloud Storage for deployment to Vertex AI Endpoints

The business wants the model to be retrained every two weeks using updated shipment data. You need to automate this retraining process while keeping cost and implementation effort low and enabling easy monitoring. What should you do?

options.text: Configure the existing notebook to include parameters for the date range and schedule it to run on a two-week interval in Vertex AI Workbench
options.explanation:
The most effective approach is to define the workflow using Kubeflow Pipelines SDK with Google Cloud executors and then schedule it with Vertex AI Pipelines. This method creates a modular, reproducible pipeline that is easy to schedule and monitor. Vertex AI Pipelines offers built-in integration with the Vertex AI ecosystem, enabling structured orchestration and ML-specific monitoring without unnecessary operational overhead. It also allows your retraining process to be automated on a fixed cadence without requiring you to maintain separate infrastructure for orchestration.

The other approaches have limitations for this requirement. Scheduling the notebook directly in Vertex AI Workbench does not offer strong monitoring or alerting and would still leave you managing manual pipeline steps. Cloud Workflows with containerized stages adds unnecessary complexity for a straightforward retraining workflow and lacks tight ML-specific integration.

TensorFlow Extended with Cloud Composer is not an optimal choice for ML pipelines because Composer is built for general workflow orchestration rather than ML-specific capabilities like tracking artifacts, managing datasets, and monitoring model performance. This can lead to more complex maintenance and reduced visibility into your ML pipeline compared to using Vertex AI Pipelines.

questions.text: Your team is creating a credit risk scoring service that returns a probability value for each loan application as it is submitted. The model relies on features stored in BigQuery, which are refreshed every 15 minutes by a scheduled query. The service must respond quickly, scale automatically with demand, and require as little operational upkeep as possible. What should you do?

options.text: Configure a Cloud Run Function to query BigQuery for features during each prediction request and send them to a Vertex AI model endpoint
options.explanation:
The most effective choice is to configure Vertex AI Feature Store to automatically load features from BigQuery and serve them directly to the model, then deploy the model on a custom Vertex AI endpoint with automatic scaling. This design keeps predictions fast by avoiding repeated queries to BigQuery during inference and reduces the need for manual updates to the feature pipeline. It also allows the service to adjust to changing traffic levels without requiring infrastructure management, ensuring low latency and minimal operational work.

The other approaches have trade-offs that make them less suitable here. Querying BigQuery on every request slows down response times and adds unnecessary query costs. Using Memorystore with Kubernetes introduces extra infrastructure to manage and does not offer the same native connection with Vertex AI services. Pushing features to Feature Store but serving the model from a Compute Engine VM limits elasticity and adds maintenance overhead. These factors make them more complex and less efficient for a low-maintenance, real-time scoring system.

questions.text: You are reviewing the Vertex AI Pipeline dashboard for a nightly training run of a production image classification model. Normally the training phase completes in about 9 hours, but last night’s run finished in just under 6 hours. The pipeline code has not been changed recently and no alerts were triggered in any Vertex AI monitoring tools. You do not have access to the production dataset for compliance reasons, but you want to quickly determine if the trained model is still valid before approving deployment. What should you do?

options.text: Upgrade to the newest TensorFlow release in the pipeline environment and run the training job again
options.explanation:
The fastest and most effective way to check whether the early completion still produced a good model is to use Vertex AI TensorBoard to examine training metrics and confirm that they follow the same convergence trends as past runs. This lets you detect if the shorter runtime was due to a valid optimization or a problem such as early stopping from bad data or misconfiguration. Reviewing pipeline input details also ensures that the correct data and parameters were used for the run. This combination of metric review and pipeline validation can be done quickly without needing direct access to production data.

The other options are less efficient or do not provide enough insight into model quality. Upgrading TensorFlow and re-running training would take significant time and might not reveal the original cause of the faster completion. Checking only the model file size does not confirm accuracy, convergence, or correctness. Requesting access to production data would delay your assessment and might violate compliance restrictions, making it impractical for rapid decision-making.

questions.text: Your team has deployed a recommendation model in Vertex AI that was trained on a dataset curated from BigQuery. The prediction service runs in a Cloud Run container and receives incoming feature values from a web application. The business wants to detect when the distribution of incoming features differs significantly from the training data and be alerted automatically, while keeping ongoing maintenance to a minimum. You also want the option to review details about any detected differences without building custom visualization tools. What should you do?

options.text: Enable request-response logging for the model endpoint and create a Looker Studio dashboard to compare incoming data with the training dataset stored in BigQuery, then schedule email reports for any anomalies found
options.explanation:
The most direct and low-maintenance approach is to configure a Vertex AI Model Monitoring job on the deployed endpoint. By connecting the monitoring job to the training dataset stored in BigQuery, Vertex AI can detect shifts in feature distribution between the training and serving data. Email alerts can be sent when skew is detected, and the Vertex AI console provides tools to review and investigate the nature of the issue without the need for custom dashboards or data pipelines. This solution is specifically designed for the problem of training-serving skew and requires minimal ongoing configuration.

The other options either add unnecessary complexity or fail to provide the built-in diagnostic capabilities of Vertex AI. Using request-response logging with Looker Studio requires creating and maintaining custom dashboards, which increases maintenance work. Building a Dataflow job with TensorFlow Data Validation introduces more components to manage and requires additional custom code for alerts and reporting. Creating a Cloud Run Function to both detect skew and trigger retraining risks unnecessary retraining events and adds operational overhead, especially without the diagnostic clarity provided by Vertex AI’s monitoring tools.
