questions.text:
You trained a regression model in Vertex AI that estimates annual customer spending for a retail company. During post-training evaluation on multiple data slices, you notice that the model consistently underestimates spending for customers older than 65. You want to address this age-related bias while maintaining similar performance on the rest of the dataset.

What should you do?

options.text: Run a correlation check between each feature and the age field, and drop features that show strong correlation in both datasets
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to examine how feature values are distributed across bucketed age groups and then fix the imbalance in the training data. If customers older than 65 are underrepresented or have systematically different feature ranges, the model can learn patterns that generalize poorly to them. By quantifying these differences per age bucket and applying targeted preprocessing such as stratified resampling, reweighting, imputation for missing values, capping outliers, or age-aware bucketing, you move the training distribution closer to what you expect at inference. This addresses the cause of the age bias and usually preserves overall offline performance because you are not discarding useful signal or forcing the model to ignore informative features

The other options do not solve the underlying problem. Dropping features that correlate with age can remove important predictors and reduce accuracy, and correlation with age does not imply those features are inappropriate to use. Changing explainability baselines only affects how attribution tools present importance and has no effect on the model’s predictions. Forcing a calibration step that matches prediction distributions above and below a threshold treats the symptom and can introduce new bias or distort well calibrated scores for other groups. These alternatives either hide the bias or damage model signal rather than correcting the feature distribution imbalance that led to the issue

options.text: Analyze the feature distributions for each bucketed age group in both the training and evaluation datasets, then apply preprocessing to correct uneven distributions
options.explanation:

questions.text: You fine-tuned a TensorFlow image classification model using Vertex AI Training with a dataset of high-resolution product photos. The final model meets your accuracy requirements in offline evaluation, but when deployed to an endpoint in Vertex AI, its prediction latency exceeds your SLA by 15ms. You want to improve inference speed while making minimal changes to the model and without requiring additional retraining. What should you do?

options.text: Retrain a smaller version of the model using the same training dataset while matching the accuracy of the fine-tuned model
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to perform post-training quantization on the already fine-tuned model and redeploy it. Quantization reduces the precision of stored weight values, which decreases model size and speeds up computation during inference. This approach can be applied directly to an existing trained model without modifying its architecture or performing another training cycle, making it a fast way to meet latency goals with minimal accuracy loss. It is particularly effective when latency reduction is needed but there is no time or budget for retraining.

The other options either require retraining or produce smaller latency benefits compared to quantization. Training a smaller model from scratch or through distillation requires new experiments, more development time, and potential performance trade-offs that are harder to control. Pruning can reduce model size but often has a smaller impact on latency, especially if the deployment hardware is optimized for dense computation. Clustering can help with model compression by reducing storage size, but it has little effect on runtime prediction latency, so it would not address the SLA issue directly.

questions.text: You are building a demand forecasting model for an e-commerce company using Vertex AI Training. The dataset has been split into training, validation, and test sets, and all three have similar distributions. You have already selected the most important input variables and trained a deep learning model. In Vertex AI Experiments, you notice that the training loss stays steady at about 0.85, while the validation loss is consistently about 0.25 higher. You want to adjust the training process to reduce overfitting and bring the two losses closer together without adding more features or changing the dataset. What should you do?

options.text: Add L2 regularization to the model
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to add L2 regularization to the model. This method adds a penalty term based on the squared values of the weights, which discourages overly complex weight values and encourages the network to learn simpler patterns. By doing this, you directly address overfitting, which is the likely cause of the higher validation loss compared to the training loss. L2 regularization can be applied without altering the learning rate, network architecture, or training duration, making it a focused adjustment that targets the source of the performance gap.

The other options are less suitable because they do not directly address the overfitting issue. Lowering the learning rate and training longer might improve stability but would not necessarily fix the disparity between training and validation loss. Increasing network depth would add complexity, which can make overfitting worse in this scenario. L1 regularization is more useful for feature selection and would have less impact here, especially since the most important features have already been selected. Increasing the number of epochs in that option could also worsen overfitting instead of reducing it.

options.text: Add L1 regularization and increase the number of training epochs
options.explanation:
The best approach here is to add L2 regularization to the model. This method adds a penalty term based on the squared values of the weights, which discourages overly complex weight values and encourages the network to learn simpler patterns. By doing this, you directly address overfitting, which is the likely cause of the higher validation loss compared to the training loss. L2 regularization can be applied without altering the learning rate, network architecture, or training duration, making it a focused adjustment that targets the source of the performance gap.

The other options are less suitable because they do not directly address the overfitting issue. Lowering the learning rate and training longer might improve stability but would not necessarily fix the disparity between training and validation loss. Increasing network depth would add complexity, which can make overfitting worse in this scenario. L1 regularization is more useful for feature selection and would have less impact here, especially since the most important features have already been selected. Increasing the number of epochs in that option could also worsen overfitting instead of reducing it.

questions.text: Your team has built a fraud detection model using Vertex AI Prediction, and it is running in production. The automated training pipeline has produced a new version of the model that passed unit tests and performance benchmarks. You want to evaluate this new model in a way that ensures uninterrupted fraud detection for customers and avoids any degradation in service during the evaluation period. What should you do?

options.text: Release the new model in the same Vertex AI endpoint and configure traffic splitting so that a small portion of prediction requests go to it, increasing traffic over time if results are acceptable
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to deploy the new model in a separate Vertex AI endpoint and run it alongside the current model using shadow deployment testing. This means both models receive identical input requests, but only the outputs from the current production model are used for actual customer-facing fraud decisions. The outputs from the new model are logged for comparison and evaluation against the current model, allowing you to analyze real-world performance without any risk to users. This approach ensures zero downtime, eliminates the possibility of degraded fraud detection during the trial period, and provides realistic performance data before deciding to replace the production model.

The other options introduce unnecessary risk because they expose real users to a model that has not yet been tested in production conditions. Traffic splitting in Vertex AI Prediction or Istio-based canary releases send live traffic to the new model, meaning some users will receive results from an unproven system. Even if the percentage is small, there is still a chance of errors or performance drops. Seldon Core with a multi-armed bandit strategy dynamically routes traffic between models, which can optimize over time but still exposes customers to potential errors and requires more complex infrastructure changes that could cause service interruptions. These methods fail to guarantee a fully controlled evaluation without impacting production service quality.

questions.text: You are a machine learning engineer for a national insurance provider. You need to set up a training pipeline for several models that handle both predicting claim amounts and classifying fraud risk. Business leaders want to clearly understand how the models make decisions, and you must launch the system into production quickly without building everything from scratch. What should you do?

options.text: Configure a Vertex AI Pipeline to run a prebuilt workflow for a hybrid linear and deep neural network model on tabular data
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to run a managed Vertex AI Pipeline using the TabNet tabular workflow because TabNet provides sequential attention, which helps make model behavior easier to explain. Vertex AI’s managed workflows are built for quick deployment and handle common ML pipeline components without requiring heavy infrastructure setup. This matches the need for interpretability and rapid productionization on tabular datasets while supporting both regression and classification tasks.

The other choices either do not emphasize interpretability or require significantly more engineering effort. The hybrid linear and deep neural network workflow focuses on generalization and memorization rather than transparency in feature usage. Cloud Composer with custom deep learning code would require designing and maintaining the entire pipeline yourself, slowing delivery. Building gradient-boosted decision tree models on GKE means provisioning and managing the infrastructure manually, which adds operational overhead and delays deployment.

questions.text: You are creating a machine learning pipeline for a video recommendation platform. The training dataset consists of millions of small thumbnail images extracted from videos. You will run the training on Vertex AI and want the images stored in a way that maximizes read performance and keeps training time low without writing a large amount of custom I/O code. What should you do?

options.text: Keep the thumbnails in Cloud Storage and load them on demand during training
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to place the thumbnails in Cloud Filestore and read them in batches from serialized data formats. Filestore provides faster access to large numbers of small files than Cloud Storage, which is important when the training job involves many I/O operations. Using serialized formats like TFRecords or WebDatasets allows the data to be streamed efficiently into the training process, which increases throughput and reduces the number of file system calls. This approach balances performance with ease of integration into existing ML pipelines.

The other choices are less efficient for this scenario. Loading each file directly from Cloud Storage results in connection setup overhead for every small object, which slows training significantly. Converting to serialized files in Cloud Storage improves performance over direct access but still does not match the read speed of Filestore in high-throughput workloads. Reading each file individually from Filestore via a mounted volume is faster than Cloud Storage but still slower than using serialized records, because it does not take advantage of bulk streaming and optimized data formats.

questions.text:
You work for a sports analytics company that generates live predictions during basketball games. The system must process events as they happen, send them through an ML model, and return the prediction to the application in near real time. The model is retrained throughout the day and each new version is stored in Cloud Storage. The workflow will include:

  • Game event data is sent as a Pub/Sub message to the processing system
  • The processing system returns a Pub/Sub message with the prediction
  • Predictions are written to BigQuery for later analysis
  • The model file in Cloud Storage is replaced frequently with updated versions

You want to keep prediction latency low while making model updates automatic and maintenance light. How should you configure the architecture?

options.text: Use a Dataflow job with the RunInference API and WatchFilePattern to detect updated models in Cloud Storage and serve predictions locally in the workers
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to use a Dataflow job with the RunInference API and WatchFilePattern to detect updated models in Cloud Storage and serve predictions locally in the workers. This keeps the model loaded close to the prediction logic, avoiding remote network calls for each request. WatchFilePattern ensures that new versions are loaded automatically without restarting the job, making frequent updates seamless and keeping latency low for predictions delivered by Pub/Sub.

The other approaches add latency or operational complexity. Calling a Vertex AI Endpoint from Dataflow adds network round-trip time to each prediction request. Loading the model fresh from Cloud Storage in a Cloud Run Function for every Pub/Sub message adds significant per-request overhead and scales poorly under heavy load. Triggering a Vertex AI Pipeline for each Pub/Sub message is not suitable for near real time scenarios because of orchestration delays and the complexity of frequent model updates.

options.text: Deploy the model to Vertex AI Endpoints and call it from a DoFn within a Dataflow job for each incoming Pub/Sub message
